ch1_1.quiz_q8: The drift velocity of electrons in a typical wire carrying a few amperes is:
ch1_1.quiz_q8_a: Close to the speed of light.
ch1_1.quiz_q8_b: About the speed of sound in the metal.
ch1_1.quiz_q8_explanation: Individual electrons drift surprisingly slowly — well under a millimetre per second. A light switch works instantly because the electric field propagates through the wire near the speed of light, pushing every electron at once; the same electron does not need to travel from the switch to the lamp.

widget.title: Current Explorer
widget.description: Drag the voltage and resistance sliders. Watch the current — and the electrons in the wire below — respond.
widget.dangerNote: That’s a lot of current — enough to melt a typical resistor or wire.
widget.hint: Notice: doubling the voltage doubles the current; halving the resistance also doubles the current. Chapter 1.2 turns this intuition into the formula I = V / R.

ch1_2.heroAriaLabel: A waterwheel turning under a controlled flow of water — the classic visual analogy for an electrical load: pressure (voltage) drives flow (current) through the wheel (resistance), and the wheel's spinning is the work done (power).
ch1_2.intro: One equation pulls the previous chapter into the rest of electronics. Voltage, current, and resistance are not independent — pick any two and the third follows. Add a fourth quantity — power — and a second equation pins it down the same way, from voltage and current. Together those two equations (and their algebraic rearrangements) explain every passive circuit in this book.
ch1_2.introPreview: We will build the equations from the water-pipe picture you already know, calculate the warmth of a real resistor, work out how long an AA cell can run a small radio, and end with a peek at efficiency — the single number that distinguishes a good amplifier from a space heater.
ch1_2.ohmIntro: In Chapter 1.1 you felt it: turning up the <voltage>voltage</voltage> raises the <current>current</current>, adding <resistance>resistance</resistance> drops it. Georg Ohm turned that feeling into an equation in 1827 — for any ordinary <resistor>resistor</resistor>, the voltage across it and the current through it are strictly proportional, and the constant of proportionality is the resistance itself:
ch1_2.ohmPlotIntro: The straight-line picture below is the fastest way to internalise what that says: the line's slope is <var>R</var> itself. Drag the resistance slider and watch the whole line tilt with it.
ch1_2.ohmRearrangementsIntro: Because <var>V</var> = <var>I</var> · <var>R</var> is a single equation in three variables, fixing any two pins down the third. The two useful rearrangements:
ch1_2.ohmRearrangeI: <var>I</var> = <var>V</var> / <var>R</var> — current when you know the voltage and the resistance.
ch1_2.ohmRearrangeR: <var>R</var> = <var>V</var> / <var>I</var> — resistance when you know the voltage and the current.
ch1_2.ohmWorkedExample: A worked example — the sixty-watt bulb. Take a 60 W incandescent bulb on a 120 V line. Voltage, current, and power are linked by P = V · I, so the current must be 60 / 120 = 0.5 A. The filament's resistance while lit then follows from Ohm's law: R = V / I = 120 / 0.5 = 240 Ω. When the bulb is cold that resistance is about 10 Ω — more than twenty times lower. Hot filaments aren't strictly ohmic, but the arithmetic is close enough for a back-of-envelope sanity check, and the law has already done real work on the very first example.
ch1_2.notationNote: A note on notation. Many older ham-radio textbooks write Ohm's law as E = I · R, using E for voltage (from electromotive force). Modern circuit-design textbooks write V = I · R. Both refer to the same voltage and the same equation. We use V here for consistency with Chapter 1.1; if you cross-reference an older book and see E, just read it as V.
ch1_2.powerIntro: Current through a resistor does not simply pass through — electrons lose a bit of energy to the lattice at every collision, and that energy leaves as heat. Power is the rate of that energy transfer, and for any circuit element the relationship is stunningly simple: voltage across it times current through it.
ch1_2.powerDerivation: Because <var>V</var>, <var>I</var>, and <var>R</var> are already tied together by Ohm's law, the same power can be written two other ways — each useful when you happen not to know all three quantities directly:
ch1_2.powerThreeFormsNote: Each form answers a different question. Use <var>P</var> = <var>V</var> · <var>I</var> when you have measured both the voltage and the current. Use <var>P</var> = <var>I</var>² · <var>R</var> when you know the current flowing through a resistor and want to predict the heat it will dissipate. Use <var>P</var> = <var>V</var>² / <var>R</var> when you know the voltage across something and want to know what it will draw.
ch1_2.powerDangerNote: A cautionary calculation. A piece of wire with 1 Ω resistance connected directly across a 12 V car battery: P = V² / R = 144 / 1 = 144 W. That is the heat of an old incandescent bulb, concentrated in a wire never meant to carry more than a few amps. Shorts across power sources melt wires and start fires — which is why every car battery has a main fuse, and why every piece of hobby gear earns one too.
ch1_2.powerLadderIntro: The <watt>watt</watt> spans many orders of magnitude in everyday life. Here is a compact ladder of power levels you meet in electronics and radio:
ch1_2.powerLadderAriaLabel: Vertical ladder of typical power levels from 20 mW (an LED indicator) up to 100 kW (a small AM broadcast station; AM = amplitude modulation), with a multiplier shown between each adjacent pair.
ch1_2.powerLadderCaption: Each rung multiplies the power by the number shown between it and the rung below. A 100 kW broadcast station draws roughly five million times the power of a single LED indicator.
ch1_2.calculatorIntro: Section 1 gave you <var>V</var> = <var>I</var> · <var>R</var>. Section 2 gave you <var>P</var> = <var>V</var> · <var>I</var> and two derived forms. Four quantities tied by two equations leave only two degrees of freedom: fix any two, and the other two are determined. The calculator below is that algebra turned into a lookup — pick any two, enter their values, and read off the remaining two with the formula used shown under each result.
ch1_2.ratingsIntro: Buying the right resistor is not only about choosing the right ohms — you also need a part that can shed the heat it will dissipate without burning out. Wattage rating is the second number on every resistor's datasheet, and it matters as soon as the calculation in §2 gives you anything above a fraction of a watt.
ch1_2.ratingsLadderTitle: Resistor wattage ratings — physical form and use
ch1_2.ratingsLadderAriaLabel: Vertical ladder of standard resistor wattage ratings from 1⁄16 W surface-mount up to 1 kW oil-cooled dummy loads, with a multiplier shown between adjacent pairs.
ch1_2.ratingsLadderCaption: Each rung roughly doubles the dissipation and — step by step — the physical size. A 1 kW antenna dummy load is about sixteen thousand times bigger, heat-wise, than the SMD resistor under an IC pull-up.
ch1_2.ratingsLadderSmd: SMD 0402 (surface-mount device) — IC pull-ups, digital signals
ch1_2.ratingsLadderQuarterW: Through-hole ¼ W — everyday hobby default
ch1_2.ratingsLadderOneW: Larger through-hole 1 W — LED strings, PSU feedback
ch1_2.ratingsLadderFiveW: Wire-wound 5 W — PSU bleeders, small current limiters
ch1_2.ratingsLadderFiftyW: Chassis-mount 50 W — small dummy loads, high-current paths
ch1_2.ratingsLadderKilowatt: Oil-cooled 1 kW+ — antenna dummy loads for HF (high-frequency, 3–30 MHz) transmitter testing
ch1_2.ratingsWidgetIntro: A practical corollary comes straight out of the two equations we just met. On a fixed supply voltage, a smaller <var>R</var> lets more current through: by <var>I</var> = <var>V</var> / <var>R</var>, halving <var>R</var> doubles <var>I</var>. And more current through the same resistor means more heat dissipated: by <var>P</var> = <var>V</var>² / <var>R</var>, halving <var>R</var> doubles <var>P</var>. Push <var>R</var> low enough and the dissipated power crosses the rating; the resistor overheats and fails. So for every (supply voltage, resistor rating) pair there is a minimum safe resistor value, below which the part burns out: <nowrap><var>R_{\mathrm{min}}</var></nowrap> = <var>V</var>² / <nowrap><var>P_{\mathrm{rated}}</var></nowrap>.
ch1_2.deratingNote: Derating — practical margin. The calculator gives the theoretical threshold R_{\mathrm{min}}: the resistance at which dissipation exactly equals the rating. In practice you want the resistor running at no more than about 50 % of its rated dissipation, less if the enclosure runs warm. So for a 12 V rail with ¼ W resistors, treat 1 kΩ (≈ 144 mW, 58 % of rating) as the practical floor, not the 576 Ω the formula allows.
ch1_2.energyIntro: Watts measure a rate — how fast energy is moving. Multiply that rate by the time over which it flows and you get energy itself:
ch1_2.energyUnitsNote: The SI unit of energy is the joule: one watt flowing for one second delivers one joule. For batteries and everyday circuits, the practical unit is the watt-hour (Wh): one watt for one hour, which works out to 3600 joules.
ch1_2.energyCapacityNote: Battery datasheets almost always list capacity in mAh (milliampere-hours) or Ah, not Wh. That capacity is an amount of electric charge, written Q, measured in coulombs: 1 Ah is 3600 C (one ampere flowing for one second carries one coulomb). The convention survives because current drain is what directly limits how long that charge lasts. Two formulas fall straight out: stored energy is voltage times charge, and runtime is charge divided by drain current.
ch1_2.energyWorkedExample: A handheld on receive. A typical handheld amateur radio covers two wavelength bands: 2 metres (the VHF amateur band, around 144 MHz) and 70 centimetres (UHF, around 435 MHz) — the two most common bands for portable ham operation. It runs from a 2100 mAh, 7.4 V lithium pack holding about 15.5 Wh of stored energy. At 150 mA of receive current, runtime is 2100 / 150 = 14 hours — enough for a full weekend on air with room to spare. Switch to transmit at a few amps and the same pack drains in under an hour, which is where efficiency (next section) starts to matter.
ch1_2.efficiencyIntro: Power going in rarely equals useful power out. A transmitter pulls DC (direct-current) power from its battery but radiates only part of it as RF (radio-frequency) through the antenna; a motor delivers less mechanical power than it draws electrically; an LED turns electrical energy into light only partially, and the rest leaves as heat. The ratio of useful output power to input power is efficiency, written with the Greek letter η (eta):
ch1_2.efficiencyExample: A representative ham transceiver draws about 100 W from its power supply but radiates around 60 W as RF — that is η ≈ 0.60, or 60 %. The remaining 40 W leaves the chassis as heat, which is why transmitters have heatsinks and cooling fans. Efficiency — and the reasons real numbers often fall short of what datasheets claim — comes back properly in Chapter 2.3, where transmitter power gets a closer look.
ch1_2.keyTakeaway: Voltage, current, and resistance are linked by <var>V</var> = <var>I</var> · <var>R</var>. Voltage and current together set the power dissipated, <var>P</var> = <var>V</var> · <var>I</var>. Power × time is energy. Every passive-circuit calculation in this book — every resistor sized, every battery life estimated, every dummy load specified — is one of these equations applied carefully.
ch1_2.labGoal: Measure the voltage across and current through a resistor powered by a single AA cell. Confirm the resistor's ohms from Ohm's law, compute the dissipated power as voltage times current, and feel the resistor warm up — the warmth IS the watts leaving the circuit as heat.
ch1_2.labComp1: One AA cell — either a 1.5 V alkaline or a 1.2 V NiMH rechargeable works
ch1_2.labComp2: One 22 Ω resistor, ¼ W rating (any tolerance)
ch1_2.labComp3: Optional: a second resistor of 47 Ω or 100 Ω for comparison
ch1_2.labStep1: Insert the 22 Ω resistor into the breadboard so its two legs land on two different rows. Connect the AA cell's + terminal to one leg and the − terminal to the other.
ch1_2.labStep2: Set the multimeter to <dc>DC</dc> volts, range 2 V (or autorange). Touch the probes to the two legs of the resistor. Record the reading — call it <var>V</var>.
ch1_2.labStep3: Set the multimeter to DC amps, range 200 mA. Break the loop between the cell and the resistor, then reconnect it with the multimeter in series so the current flows through the meter. Record the reading — call it <var>I</var>.
ch1_2.labStep4: Compute <var>R</var> = <var>V</var> / <var>I</var>. You should get a value close to the marked 22 Ω — within about 5 %, plus whatever tolerance band the resistor has.
ch1_2.labStep5: Compute <var>P</var> = <var>V</var> · <var>I</var>. With a fresh AA cell this lands around 90–100 mW — well under the resistor's ¼ W (250 mW) rating.
ch1_2.labStep6: Leave the circuit powered for about thirty seconds, then gently touch the resistor. It should feel distinctly warm. That warmth IS the <var>P</var> = <var>V</var> · <var>I</var> milliwatts you just computed, leaving as heat.
ch1_2.labExpected: Voltage around 1.4 V, current around 60–65 mA, resistance close to 22 Ω, power around 90 mW. The resistor is noticeably warm to the touch after half a minute.
ch1_2.labConnection: The warmth is not a side effect — it is exactly what the resistor does with the electrical power. Every milliwatt entering the resistor comes out as heat. That is what 'dissipation' means, and why the power rating on a resistor's datasheet is the single most important number after the ohms.
ch1_2.labTrouble1: Multimeter reads 0 V or 0 mA — check that the AA cell is in the holder the right way round and that the jumper wires are actually making contact with both terminals.
ch1_2.labTrouble2: Multimeter display flashes or goes blank when inserted in series — the probes are in the amp jacks but the rotary dial is in volts mode (or vice versa). Set the dial correctly BEFORE touching the probes to the circuit.
ch1_2.labTrouble3: Resistor heats up very quickly or starts to smell — you have a resistor with a lower value than 22 Ω. Disconnect the battery immediately and double-check the colour bands.
ch1_2.kirchhoffIntro: Ohm's law applies to a single resistor: it binds voltage, current, and resistance together through one equation. Real circuits branch and loop into networks of many resistors and many junctions, and two more laws, discovered by Gustav Kirchhoff in 1845, complete the toolkit:
ch1_2.kirchhoffKcl: Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) — at any junction, the currents flowing in equal the currents flowing out. Charge does not pile up at a point.
ch1_2.kirchhoffKvl: Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) — around any closed loop in a circuit, the voltage rises and drops sum to zero. The voltage rise produced by the source is balanced by the voltage drops across every other element in the loop.
ch1_2.kirchhoffClose: Combined with V = I · R and P = V · I, these four rules are enough to analyse every passive resistor network. We put them to work in Chapter 1.4, where series and parallel combinations get their own section.
ch1_2.quiz_q1: A 9 V battery is connected across a 330 Ω resistor. What current flows through the resistor?
ch1_2.quiz_q1_explanation: By Ohm's law <nowrap>I = V / R = 9 / 330 ≈ 0.027 A</nowrap>, or about 27 mA. Getting the prefix right matters as much as the formula — the other three options differ from the correct answer only by a factor of 1000.
ch1_2.quiz_q2: You know the current through a resistor (0.1 A) and its resistance (47 Ω). Which formula gives the dissipated power directly from those two values?
ch1_2.quiz_q2_explanation: With I and R known, <nowrap>P = I² · R</nowrap> plugs in directly: <nowrap>0.01 · 47 = 0.47 W</nowrap>. The other two power forms would force you to compute V first. <nowrap>P = V · Q</nowrap> is an energy relation, not a power relation.
ch1_2.quiz_q3: A circuit places 12 V across a resistor. What is the MINIMUM resistance that keeps the dissipated power within the rating of a ¼ W resistor?
ch1_2.quiz_q3_explanation: <nowrap><var>R_{\mathrm{min}}</var> = V² / <var>P_{\mathrm{rated}}</var> = 144 / 0.25 = 576 Ω</nowrap>. Below 576 Ω the resistor would dissipate more than 0.25 W and overheat. In practice you would then apply a derating margin on top — picking roughly twice that (1152 Ω or higher) leaves the resistor room to run cool.
ch1_2.quiz_q4: A transmitter draws 100 W from its power supply and radiates 60 W as RF. What is its efficiency?
ch1_2.quiz_q4_explanation: <nowrap>η = <var>P_{\mathrm{out}}</var> / <var>P_{\mathrm{in}}</var> = 60 / 100 = 0.60</nowrap>, or 60 %. The remaining 40 W leaves the chassis as heat — which is why transmitters have heatsinks and cooling fans.
ch1_2.quiz_q5: A 2000 mAh battery supplies a steady 50 mA load. Approximately how long will it run?
ch1_2.quiz_q5_explanation: <nowrap>t = Q / I = 2000 mAh / 50 mA = 40 h</nowrap>. That is the datasheet ceiling — real batteries typically deliver 80–90 % of it because voltage sags under load and some charge is unreachable at that discharge rate.
ch1_2.quiz_q6: Three wires meet at a junction. 2 A flows IN on the first wire, 1.5 A flows OUT on the second. What does Kirchhoff's current law (KCL) say about the third wire?
ch1_2.quiz_q6_explanation: KCL: at any junction, currents in = currents out. 2 A flowing in must equal 1.5 A + X flowing out, so X = 0.5 A flowing out. Charge does not pile up at a point.
ch1_2.quiz_q7: A 9 V source drives two resistors in series. The voltage across the first resistor measures 5.5 V. What is the voltage across the second resistor?
ch1_2.quiz_q7_explanation: KVL: the voltage rise from the source (9 V) is balanced by the voltage drops across every passive element in the loop. <nowrap>9 = 5.5 + X</nowrap>, so the second resistor drops 3.5 V.
ch1_2.quiz_q8: A 100 Ω resistor carries 0.1 A. After a minute it feels warm to the touch. Which statement best explains why?
ch1_2.quiz_q8_a: Resistors give off heat regardless of current.
ch1_2.quiz_q8_b: The resistor dissipates <nowrap>I² · R = 1 W</nowrap> — every watt of electrical power entering the resistor comes out as heat.
ch1_2.quiz_q8_c: The battery is slowly discharging and heating the whole circuit.
ch1_2.quiz_q8_d: The resistor should not be heating up — the colour-band value was probably read wrong.
ch1_2.quiz_q8_explanation: Dissipation IS what a resistor does with electrical power: <nowrap>P = I² · R = 0.01 · 100 = 1 W</nowrap> comes out as heat. A 1 W dissipation on a typical ¼ W resistor would also exceed its rating — which is exactly why power ratings matter in the first place.

ohmPlot.title: Ohm's law — the V–I line
ohmPlot.description: Drag the resistance slider. The line's slope is R: bigger R, steeper line. The relationship is always straight — that is what "linear" means in Ohm's law.
ohmPlot.ariaLabel: Plot of voltage against current. A straight line through the origin whose slope equals the selected resistance.
ohmPlot.hint: Notice the line always passes through the origin — zero current through a resistor means zero voltage across it, and vice versa. Doubling the current on any line doubles the voltage. In Chapter 1.4, diodes will give you your first non-straight V–I curve; that is when "linear" stops being free.

ohmCalc.title: Ohm's law + power calculator
ohmCalc.description: Pick any two of V, I, R, P as your known inputs. The calculator derives the other two and shows the formula used under each result.
ohmCalc.hint: Change either dropdown to swap in a different known quantity. Units auto-scale on results (0.0255 A renders as 25.5 mA). Dividing by zero, or √ of a negative, shows a dash instead of a number.

safeZone.title: Minimum-safe resistor calculator
safeZone.description: Pick a supply voltage and a resistor's wattage rating. The calculator gives the smallest resistor value you can put across that supply without the rating being exceeded.
safeZone.hint: Any resistor whose resistance value is above this threshold is safe on the chosen supply. A resistor with resistance below this threshold will dissipate more power than the rating can handle, heat up, and eventually fail. See the derating note above for the practical margin.

runtime.title: Battery runtime calculator
runtime.description: Enter a battery's capacity, nominal voltage, and the load's current drain. The calculator shows the total stored energy and how long the battery will last at that drain.
runtime.hint: Runtime = capacity ÷ drain; the nominal voltage is used only to compute stored energy (V · Q). Real batteries deliver ~80–90 % of this in practice — voltage sags under load and some charge is inaccessible at the rated discharge rate — but this is the headline figure the datasheet promises.

ch1_3.heroAriaLabel: An oscilloscope screen showing two traces on a shared time axis: a flat horizontal line labelled DC (direct current) in the upper half, and a sine wave labelled AC (alternating current) in the lower half. A small time arrow under the bottom-right corner reminds the reader that the horizontal axis is time.
ch1_3.intro: Until now every voltage and current in this book has been a single number. 1.5 V across an AA cell, 27 mA through a resistor — quantities that sit still while we measure them. Real electronics rarely holds that still. Mains power swings up and down fifty times a second, radio signals oscillate at millions of cycles a second, and your voice leaving a microphone is a waveform that never repeats exactly. Time is the axis that was missing; this chapter puts it back.
ch1_3.introPreview: We will split current into two kinds — direct (flowing steadily in one direction) and alternating (periodically changing direction), meet the simplest repeating waveform and the small set of numbers that describe it, pick up four different ways to measure its «size», and answer the one question that trips up every new ham and hobbyist: if a voltage is changing fifty times a second, what exactly does the number on a multimeter mean?
ch1_3.sectionSources: DC and AC — two kinds of current
ch1_3.sourcesIntro: <dc>DC</dc> (direct current) is current that flows steadily in one direction. Batteries produce DC. Solar panels in sunlight produce DC. The 5 V rail a USB socket delivers is DC. Plotted on an oscilloscope, a steady DC voltage is a flat horizontal line — nothing is changing with time.
ch1_3.sourcesAcIntro: <ac>AC</ac> (alternating current) reverses direction many times a second. The wall socket in your room is AC — its voltage swings up, past zero, to the opposite polarity, back through zero, and repeats, fifty or sixty times a second. The radio wave arriving at an antenna is AC, oscillating millions of times a second. The audio voltage a microphone produces is AC. Plotted on an oscilloscope, AC signals are curves that swing above and below zero.
ch1_3.sourcesWhy: AC dominates two things that DC cannot do well. First, long-distance power distribution: AC voltages can be stepped up and down by transformers with almost no loss, so power stations can send electricity hundreds of kilometres at hundreds of thousands of volts and bring it down to 230 V for your house. Second, radio: an electromagnetic wave only radiates if charges are accelerating — which means the current driving the antenna must be changing in time — and what exactly those changes look like is the subject of the next section.
ch1_3.sourcesBridge: Most circuits mix the two. A transmitter runs on DC from its power supply but generates AC at radio frequencies. A receiver pulls AC from the antenna, mixes and filters it, and extracts DC to drive a speaker coil whose motion we hear as AC acoustic waves in the air. Understanding both kinds of current, and the boundary between them, is the single most useful thing this chapter will give you.
ch1_3.sectionSineWave: The sine wave — the fundamental shape of radio signals
ch1_3.sineWaveIntro: Any waveform that repeats can be built up from <sine>sine waves</sine>; the sine is the natural shape of any linear oscillation. A mass on a spring traces one. A swinging pendulum (small angles) traces one. An <lc>LC</lc> circuit — a <cap>capacitor</cap> and an <ind>inductor</ind> tied together, which we meet properly in Chapter 1.7 — rings at a pure sine. The mains, the carrier of every radio transmission, and the test signal every bench instrument generates: all sines, because the physics that produces them is linear.
ch1_3.sineWaveThreeProps: A pure sine is fully described by three numbers: its amplitude (how far it swings from the centre line to a peak), its frequency (how many cycles it completes per second, in hertz), and its phase (where along its cycle it is at t = 0). A fourth quantity — the period T — is just the reciprocal of the frequency: the time one full cycle takes.
ch1_3.sineWaveFormula: Putting that into an equation, a sine voltage of amplitude <var>A</var> and frequency <var>f</var>, starting from zero at <var>t</var> = 0, is:
ch1_3.sineWavePeriodNote: Frequency and period hold hands: a 1 Hz sine repeats every 1 second; a 1 kHz (1000 Hz) sine repeats every 1 ms; a 100 MHz FM broadcast signal repeats every 10 nanoseconds. Low frequency = long period; high frequency = short period.
ch1_3.sineWaveExplorerIntro: The explorer below plots this equation on a 20 ms time window. Drag the amplitude slider and the trace grows or shrinks vertically; drag the frequency slider and more or fewer cycles pack into the same window. The amplitude (<var>A</var>) and period (<var>T</var>) arrows update in real time so the two numerical readouts below the plot line up with something visible on screen.
ch1_3.levelsIntro: Given a sine wave that swings up and down, what single number should represent its 'size'? There are four reasonable answers, each measuring something slightly different, and each has a name every ham has to know on sight.
ch1_3.levelsPeak: Peak voltage (V_{\mathrm{pk}}) is the amplitude — the distance from zero to the highest excursion. A sine with peaks at +10 V and −10 V has V_{\mathrm{pk}} = 10 V. Capacitor datasheets quote maximum voltage as a peak value: the peak is what, for a brief instant each cycle, stresses the dielectric the most.
ch1_3.levelsPeakToPeak: Peak-to-peak voltage (V_{\mathrm{pp}}) is twice the peak — the full vertical span of the waveform. That same sine has V_{\mathrm{pp}} = 20 V. Oscilloscopes report peak-to-peak most naturally because you can read it directly off the screen by counting grid divisions from the lowest point to the highest.
ch1_3.levelsAverage: Average voltage (V_{\mathrm{avg}}) over a full cycle is zero — the positive half exactly cancels the negative half. Simply averaging the voltage of a sine wave therefore tells you nothing useful: you always get zero. In practice the useful average is the rectified average, over one half cycle, which works out to 2/π ≈ 0.637 of the peak. That is the quantity a cheap multimeter actually measures in its AC mode.
ch1_3.levelsRmsIntro: RMS voltage (V_{\mathrm{rms}}) — short for root-mean-square — is the number that ties an AC signal back to DC power: it is the DC voltage that would dissipate the same average power in a resistor as the AC waveform does. For a pure sine, the arithmetic works out to:
ch1_3.levelsRmsFormula: So a sine with peaks at ±10 V has <var>V_{\mathrm{rms}}</var> ≈ 7.07 V. Plug that 10 V-peak sine into a 10 Ω resistor and it heats up the same as a 7.07 V DC source would. When a heating element or a transmitter is rated in watts, the watts are computed from RMS voltage and RMS current — it is the only AC number for which the DC power formulas (<var>P</var> = <var>V</var> · <var>I</var>, <var>P</var> = <var>V</var>² / <var>R</var>) still work.
ch1_3.rmsSelectorIntro: The widget below fixes a sine at 10 V peak and lets you pick which 'size' is drawn on top of it. Watch how each level sits at a different height on the same waveform — peak-to-peak spans the whole thing, peak reaches half that, RMS lands about 71 % of the way up, and the rectified average sits at roughly 64 %. The numeric readout lists all four at once so you can see the relationships without switching back and forth.
ch1_3.rmsSelectorTakeaway: Moving between those four numbers is an arithmetic step that every radio and audio engineer does dozens of times a day. The conversions worth memorising: <nowrap><var>V_{\mathrm{pp}}</var> = 2 · <var>V_{\mathrm{pk}}</var></nowrap>, and for a sine <nowrap><var>V_{\mathrm{rms}}</var> = <var>V_{\mathrm{pk}}</var> / √2 = 0.707 · <var>V_{\mathrm{pk}}</var></nowrap>, and <nowrap><var>V_{\mathrm{pk}}</var> = 1.414 · <var>V_{\mathrm{rms}}</var></nowrap>.
ch1_3.sectionMains: Mains voltage — the canonical worked example
ch1_3.mainsIntro: Every spec sheet for a <mains>mains</mains>-powered appliance quotes voltage as an RMS value — and only as RMS. That is the number that matters for heating power and motor torque. But the capacitors, insulators, and switching transistors inside the appliance see the peak voltage, which is <nowrap>√2 larger</nowrap>. Getting those two numbers straight is the difference between a working power supply and a capacitor that fails in a puff of acrid smoke.
ch1_3.mainsWorkedEurope: Europe, Ukraine, UK — 230 V RMS at 50 Hz. Peak voltage: V_{\mathrm{pk}} = 1.414 × 230 ≈ 325 V. Peak-to-peak: 650 V. Period of one cycle: T = 1 / 50 Hz = 20 ms. So any capacitor connected directly across the mains needs to be rated for at least 325 V, ideally 400 V for margin. Electrolytics rated "250 V" are suitable only for DC buses inside equipment, never across the raw mains.
ch1_3.mainsWorkedUsa: USA, parts of Latin America, Japan — 120 V RMS at 60 Hz. (Japan runs 100 V.) Peak voltage: V_{\mathrm{pk}} = 1.414 × 120 ≈ 170 V. Peak-to-peak: 340 V. Period: T = 1 / 60 Hz ≈ 16.7 ms. The lower RMS is why US appliances draw twice the current for the same wattage — a 1500 W kettle pulls 6.5 A in Europe but 12.5 A in the US.
ch1_3.mainsCapacitorCallout: A classic mistake. A hobbyist buys a 220 µF, 250 V electrolytic capacitor and connects it directly across the 230 V mains to smooth something. The cap is fine on paper: 230 V is less than 250 V. But the mains is AC — 325 V peak. Every half-cycle (10 ms) the mains voltage reverses polarity. For one half it pushes the capacitor the wrong way round — and electrolytics are not designed to tolerate voltage in the reverse direction at all. For the other half the polarity is correct, but the peak voltage (325 V) exceeds the capacitor’s rating (250 V). It fails — usually violently — within minutes. Mains-rated filter capacitors are always specified as peak voltage, and always include a healthy margin (400 V or 630 V are the common values for 230 V mains work).
ch1_3.sectionNonSine: Non-sinusoidal waves — where cheap meters lie
ch1_3.nonSineIntro: The formulas we have derived for RMS and the rectified average (<nowrap><var>V_{\mathrm{rms}}</var> = <var>V_{\mathrm{pk}}</var> / √2</nowrap> and <nowrap><var>V_{\mathrm{avg}}</var> = 0.637 · <var>V_{\mathrm{pk}}</var></nowrap>) only hold for a pure sine. Real signals are often not pure sines. A PWM output from an Arduino is a <square>square wave</square>. Triangle waves come from a lab signal generator. The voltage coming out of a cheap class-D amplifier is a rapidly-switched square wave, only partially smoothed by a filter. The relationship between the peak, average, and RMS values depends on the shape of the waveform.
ch1_3.nonSineGalleryIntro: The gallery below compares a sine, a square, and a triangle — all normalised to a peak amplitude of 1. Three numbers sit under each trace: its true RMS value, its <formFactor>form factor</formFactor> (RMS divided by rectified average — a pure-shape quantity), and what a cheap averaging DMM would read on it. The averaging DMM assumes it is looking at a sine and multiplies the rectified average by 1.11 (the form factor of a rectified sine) to get an 'RMS' reading. That trick works perfectly for sines and gives the wrong answer for everything else:
ch1_3.nonSineErrors: Square wave: true RMS is 1.00 (equal to peak); cheap meter reads 1.11 — 11 % high. Triangle wave: true RMS is 1/√3 ≈ 0.577; cheap meter reads 0.555 — 3.8 % low. Sine wave: cheap meter reads 0.707, which is the correct RMS — by design.
ch1_3.trueRmsNote: When accuracy matters, look for "True RMS" printed on your multimeter's front panel. A true-RMS meter digitises the waveform and computes the actual root-mean-square; it reads correctly on any shape. Cheap and mid-range DMMs are usually averaging-type; bench-grade and professional DMMs are almost always true-RMS. For audio, switched-mode power supplies, PWM, chopped mains dimmers, and anything non-sinusoidal, only a true-RMS meter gives you a believable number.
ch1_3.sectionForward: What’s next — AC in real circuits
ch1_3.forwardReactance: Capacitors and inductors pass AC and DC differently. A capacitor blocks DC completely (after the initial charge-up) but passes AC — and the higher the frequency, the more easily it passes. An inductor does the opposite: it passes DC freely and opposes AC, more strongly at higher frequencies. That frequency-dependent opposition is called reactance, and it is the subject of Chapters 1.5 and 1.6.
ch1_3.forwardHarmonics: Any repeating waveform is a sum of sines. A square wave is a sine at the fundamental frequency plus smaller sines at three times, five times, seven times, and so on — these higher multiples are called harmonics. A triangle is the same with a different mix. That insight — called Fourier analysis — turns every complicated signal into a list of frequencies, and is why the filters we build in Chapter 1.8 let some frequencies through and block others. The decomposition is also what lets a radio transmitter generate a square or triangle test signal, filter out everything but the fundamental, and have a clean sine carrier at the end.

sineExplorer.title: Sine wave — amplitude, frequency, period
sineExplorer.description: Drive the amplitude (A) and frequency (f) sliders. The plot updates live; the A and T arrows on the waveform show you each quantity as a distance you can measure with a ruler on the screen.
sineExplorer.ariaLabel: A sine wave plotted against time over a 20 ms window, with amplitude and period markers that update with the sliders.
sineExplorer.hint: The amplitude and period arrows are tied to the equation V(t) = A · sin(2π · f · t). Changing A scales the vertical arrow; changing f stretches or shrinks the horizontal one. The frequency slider is log-scaled so doubling the frequency covers the same slider distance at any starting value.

rmsSelector.title: Peak · peak-to-peak · average · RMS
rmsSelector.description: The waveform is a fixed 10 V-peak, 100 Hz sine. Pick a level below and a dashed reference line of that value is overlaid on the trace.
rmsSelector.ariaLabel: A 10 V-peak 100 Hz sine wave with a selectable reference level overlaid — peak, peak-to-peak, average, or RMS.
rmsSelector.hint_peak: Peak is the amplitude: the distance from zero to the highest excursion. It is what stresses a capacitor's dielectric for one instant per cycle.
rmsSelector.hint_pp: Peak-to-peak is the total vertical span of the waveform, from the lowest point to the highest. It is what you read most naturally off an oscilloscope screen.
rmsSelector.hint_avg: Over a full cycle the true average is zero — the positive and negative halves cancel. The number shown is the half-cycle rectified average (2/π · <var>V_{\mathrm{pk}}</var>), which is what an averaging-type multimeter actually responds to.
rmsSelector.hint_rms: RMS is the DC voltage that would dissipate the same average power in a resistor as the AC waveform does. For a pure sine, <var>V_{\mathrm{rms}}</var> = <var>V_{\mathrm{pk}}</var> / √2 ≈ 0.707 · <var>V_{\mathrm{pk}}</var>. It is the only AC 'size' for which the DC power formulas still work without modification.

waveformGallery.sineAria: A sine wave over two full cycles, centred on the zero line.
waveformGallery.squareAria: A bipolar square wave over two full cycles, switching sharply between +peak and −peak.
waveformGallery.triangleAria: A symmetric triangle wave over two full cycles, ramping linearly between +peak and −peak.
waveformGallery.caption: Three waveforms, all with peak amplitude 1. True RMS and form factor are pure properties of the shape; the averaging-DMM row shows what a cheap meter would display, which equals the true RMS only for sines (green). On square and triangle waves the meter reads wrong (orange).

ch1_3.labGoal: See for yourself how your multimeter's DC and AC modes behave on three different sources — a battery, a USB supply, and an Arduino PWM pin — and watch the AC reading reveal the switching component that DC mode averages away.
ch1_3.labEquip3: <arduino>Arduino</arduino> Uno (or any Arduino-compatible board with PWM pins)
ch1_3.labComp1: One AA cell in a holder — 1.5 V alkaline or 1.2 V NiMH is fine
ch1_3.labComp3: One phone USB charger (any make — just plugged in as a 5 V DC source)
ch1_3.labStep1: AA cell on DC, then AC. Set the multimeter to DC volts (2 V range or autorange). Touch the probes across the AA cell — you should read somewhere around 1.5 V. Now switch the meter to AC volts and probe the same cell. The reading should collapse to near zero (at most a few millivolts of meter noise). A DC source has no alternating component.
ch1_3.labStep2: USB charger — same two modes. Plug your USB charger in. Find its +5 V and GND pins (or use a USB breakout). Measure on DC — about 5 V. Switch to AC — should again read near zero, because the charger's internal filter capacitors have smoothed its output. If you do read a millivolt or two of AC, that is ripple — the small remaining AC component of imperfectly-filtered DC.
ch1_3.labStep3: Arduino PWM. Upload a one-line sketch: void setup() { analogWrite(9, 128); } void loop() {}. That drives pin 9 with a ~490 Hz square wave at 50 % duty cycle. Connect pin 9 through the 10 kΩ resistor to GND on the breadboard.
ch1_3.labStep4: Probe pin 9 to GND on DC. You should read somewhere near 2.5 V — the average of the 0 V / 5 V square wave at 50 % duty cycle. The multimeter's integration time (~100–300 ms) smooths the switching out completely.
ch1_3.labStep5: Switch to AC volts. Now the meter does not read zero — it shows a voltage reflecting the switching component. The exact number depends on your meter (cheap DMMs are calibrated for sines and do not have the bandwidth to measure 490 Hz accurately), but the lesson is qualitative: DC mode sees the average, AC mode sees the swing.
ch1_3.labStep6: Change the duty cycle. Change the sketch to analogWrite(9, 64) (25 % duty) — DC reading drops to ~1.25 V. Try analogWrite(9, 255) (100 % duty, i.e. continuous high) — DC reads ~5 V and the AC reading collapses to zero, because a constant voltage is pure DC again.
ch1_3.labExpected: AA cell: 1.5 V DC, ~0 V AC. USB: 5 V DC, ~0 V AC. Arduino PWM at 50 %: ~2.5 V DC, a non-zero but meter-dependent AC reading. DC reading tracks the duty cycle linearly; AC reading peaks around 50 % duty and drops to zero at 0 % and 100 %.
ch1_3.labConnection: A multimeter's DC mode averages; its AC mode measures what is left after blocking the DC average (internally via a capacitor). DC + AC together describe the whole waveform. This is the same decomposition every oscilloscope, every spectrum analyser, and every radio front end relies on — and the reason cheap multimeters give misleading numbers on PWM: they assume whatever they are measuring is a sine.
ch1_3.labTrouble1: AC reading on the AA cell is not zero (a few tens of millivolts) — you are probably near a strong AC field (fluorescent lamp ballast, LED driver, phone charger, laptop PSU). Move away and retest. If it persists on a fresh battery, your meter's zero calibration may be off.
ch1_3.labTrouble2: Arduino DC reading does not change with duty cycle — check that you uploaded the sketch (not just saved it), and that the pin number in the code matches the pin you are probing. On the Uno, PWM is available on pins 3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11.
ch1_3.labTrouble3: AC reading on Arduino PWM is erratic or jumps around — cheap DMMs have a narrow AC bandwidth (often specified only from 40 Hz to 400 Hz) and cannot accurately sample a 490 Hz square wave. Treat the reading as qualitative, not a precise RMS value.
ch1_3.quiz_q1: A sine wave has a peak voltage of 20 V. What is its peak-to-peak voltage?
ch1_3.quiz_q1_explanation: Peak-to-peak is twice the peak amplitude: <nowrap><var>V_{\mathrm{pp}}</var> = 2 · <var>V_{\mathrm{pk}}</var> = 2 · 20 = 40 V</nowrap>. Peak-to-peak spans the full vertical excursion of the waveform, from its lowest point (−20 V) to its highest (+20 V).
ch1_3.quiz_q2: What is the RMS voltage of a pure sine wave whose peak amplitude is 100 V?
ch1_3.quiz_q2_explanation: For a sine, <nowrap><var>V_{\mathrm{rms}}</var> = <var>V_{\mathrm{pk}}</var> / √2 = 100 / 1.414 ≈ 70.7 V</nowrap>. That is the DC voltage that would dissipate the same average power in a resistor as the 100 V-peak sine does. 141 V is the trap — the inverse conversion (peak from RMS) rather than RMS from peak.
ch1_3.quiz_q3: Standard mains in Europe and Ukraine is quoted as 230 V. What is the peak voltage that capacitors and insulators actually see?
ch1_3.quiz_q3_explanation: Mains is quoted as RMS. <nowrap>Peak = 1.414 × 230 ≈ 325 V</nowrap>. 650 V is the peak-to-peak (<nowrap>2 × peak</nowrap>). This is why capacitors connected across mains need to be rated for at least 325 V (in practice, 400 V for margin) — never the nominal 230 V.
ch1_3.quiz_q4: A 1 kHz sine wave has what period?
ch1_3.quiz_q4_explanation: Period and frequency are reciprocals: T = 1 / f = 1 / 1000 Hz = 0.001 s = 1 ms. A higher frequency means a shorter period, and vice versa.
ch1_3.quiz_q5: Your multimeter shows 1.5 V on an AA cell in DC mode, then 0 V in AC mode. What does this tell you?
ch1_3.quiz_q5_b: A DC source has no alternating component — this is expected.
ch1_3.quiz_q5_d: The reading is suspicious — both modes should show 5 V.
ch1_3.quiz_q5_explanation: An AC voltmeter blocks the DC component (internally via a capacitor) and measures what is left swinging. A perfect DC source has nothing left to measure, so AC mode correctly shows zero. Any non-zero AC reading on a nominally-DC source would be ripple — the remaining AC component of imperfectly-filtered DC.
ch1_3.quiz_q6: A cheap (averaging-type) multimeter measures a pure square wave whose peak-to-peak swing is 2 V. The true RMS value is 1 V. What is the meter likely to display?
ch1_3.quiz_q6_a: 0.71 V (it assumes the wave is a sine)
ch1_3.quiz_q6_c: 1.11 V (it scales the rectified average by 1.11)
ch1_3.quiz_q6_d: 0.64 V (it displays the rectified average directly)
ch1_3.quiz_q6_explanation: An averaging DMM rectifies, averages, and multiplies by 1.11 (the form factor of a rectified sine) to convert to an assumed-sine RMS. On a square wave, rectified average equals the peak (1 V), so the meter displays 1.00 × 1.11 = 1.11 V — 11 % high. Only a true-RMS meter reads the correct 1.00 V.
ch1_3.quiz_q7: Which of these is NOT one of the three numbers that fully describe a pure sine wave?
ch1_3.quiz_q7_explanation: A sine is fully described by amplitude, frequency, and phase. Period is not an independent fourth number — it is just 1/frequency, so once you know the frequency you know the period.
ch1_3.quiz_q8: Why is AC used for long-distance power transmission, not DC?
ch1_3.quiz_q8_b: AC voltages can be efficiently stepped up and down by transformers, which DC cannot.
ch1_3.quiz_q8_c: DC is dangerous but AC is safe.
ch1_3.quiz_q8_explanation: Transformers work only on AC — they rely on a changing magnetic field. Stepping up to hundreds of kilovolts for transmission drops the current for the same power, which cuts resistive losses in the wires (<var>P_{\mathrm{loss}}</var> = I²·R). At the other end, transformers step back down to 230 V for your house. DC can be transmitted at high voltage too (HVDC links exist), but the conversion equipment is much more expensive than passive transformers, so AC dominates ordinary distribution.
ch1_3.sineWaveOrigin: Where the sine comes from. Picture a point moving uniformly around a circle. If you track only its vertical position — how far above or below the centre of the circle it sits at each moment — and plot that position against time, the curve you trace out is a smooth, symmetric wave. That is a sine wave. The name comes from the trigonometric function sin, which gives the height of a point on a unit circle as a function of the rotation angle. This is not a metaphor — it is the physics. Any linear oscillation (a pendulum at small angles, a mass on a spring, the ringing of an LC circuit) is, at its core, the projection of rotational motion onto a line, and so it naturally takes the shape of a sine.
ch1_3.sineWaveFormulaExplain: Breaking the expression into its parts: A is the amplitude — the radius of the imaginary circle, and the largest value the voltage reaches. f is the frequency — how many full rotations the point makes per second. 2π · f · t is the rotation angle at time t, in radians: one full rotation equals 2π radians (about 6.28), which is a different unit from 360° but more convenient for formulas. And sin(·) is the sine function, which turns an angle into the corresponding height of a point on a unit circle (between −1 and +1). In plain words, the formula says: the voltage at time t equals the height of a point that has been rotating around a circle of radius A at a rate of f turns per second.

sineOrigin.ariaLabel: An illustration showing how a sine wave is generated. On the left, a point P rotates around a circle of radius A. On the right, the height of P is plotted against time, tracing out the sine wave.
sineOrigin.caption: The sine wave is the height of a uniformly rotating point plotted against time. One full rotation of the point corresponds to one full cycle of the sine.

ch1_3.levelsRmsDerivation: Where the √2 comes from. Instantaneous power in a resistor is proportional to the square of voltage: p(t) = V(t)² / R. For a sine of amplitude V_{\mathrm{pk}} that means p(t) = V_{\mathrm{pk}}² · sin²(2π · f · t) / R. The key step: the average of sin²(θ) over a full period is exactly ½. That follows from the identity sin²(θ) + cos²(θ) = 1 — the two functions have the same shape and differ only by a shift, and their sum is always 1, so each must average to ½. Therefore the average power dissipated is V_{\mathrm{pk}}² / (2R). RMS is defined as the DC voltage that would dissipate the same average power in the same resistor: V_{\mathrm{rms}}² / R = V_{\mathrm{pk}}² / (2R). Cancelling R and taking the square root gives V_{\mathrm{rms}} = V_{\mathrm{pk}} / √2.
ch1_3.levelsAverageDerivation: Where 2/π comes from. The average of a function over an interval is «area under the curve ÷ width of the interval». For one half-cycle of a sine the width is π. The area can be bracketed without heavy math: a triangular wave of the same height 1 and base π would have area π/2 ≈ 1.57; a rectangle of the same dimensions would have area π ≈ 3.14. The sine is a smooth dome sitting between these shapes, and its exact area equals 2 (this identity comes from integration). So the average over one half-cycle equals 2/π ≈ 0.637 of the peak — roughly 64 %.
ch1_3.keyTakeaway1: DC is current that flows in one direction; AC periodically changes direction.
ch1_3.keyTakeaway2: A pure sine wave is described by three numbers — amplitude, frequency, phase — with period <nowrap><var>T</var> = 1/<var>f</var></nowrap>. Its four «sizes» are peak, peak-to-peak, rectified average, and RMS; for a sine <nowrap><var>V_{\mathrm{rms}}</var> = <var>V_{\mathrm{pk}}</var> / √2 ≈ 0.707 · <var>V_{\mathrm{pk}}</var></nowrap>.
ch1_3.keyTakeaway3: RMS is what lets you compute power as though AC were DC: <nowrap><var>P</var> = <var>V_{\mathrm{rms}}</var> · <var>I_{\mathrm{rms}}</var></nowrap>. Mains appliances are rated in RMS; their insides see the peak, which is <nowrap>√2 larger</nowrap>.
ch1_3.keyTakeaway4: Cheap multimeters are calibrated for sine waves only and read wrong on square and triangle waves — when you can afford the upgrade, a true-RMS meter is worth the extra cost.

ch1_4.heroAriaLabel: A gallery of three resistor packages drawn side by side: a through-hole axial resistor with four coloured bands in the centre, a small rectangular surface-mount chip labelled "472", and a round rotary potentiometer with a three-terminal symbol underneath. Each package is labelled with its typical size.
ch1_4.seriesParallelAria: Two side-by-side schematics. On the left, two resistors drawn in series between two terminals with a formula R equals R-one plus R-two underneath. On the right, the same two resistors drawn in parallel between the same two terminals with a formula R equals R-one times R-two divided by R-one plus R-two.
ch1_4.dividerSchematicAria: A voltage-divider schematic. A horizontal <var>V_{\mathrm{in}}</var> rail at the top connects via a resistor <var>R_{1}</var> down to a middle-point junction. From that junction a second resistor <var>R_{2}</var> continues down to a bottom rail labelled 0 V (ground). Three labelled nodes: A at the top of <var>R_{1}</var> (on the <var>V_{\mathrm{in}}</var> rail), B at the <var>R_{1}</var> – <var>R_{2}</var> junction, and C at the bottom of <var>R_{2}</var> (on the ground rail). A voltmeter (circle with a V inside) is connected in parallel with <var>R_{2}</var> — its upper lead goes to node B, its lower lead to node C — so the voltmeter's reading is <var>V_{\mathrm{out}}</var> by construction.
ch1_4.dividerSchematicCaption: The voltage divider. Three nodes marked A, B, C. A voltmeter is connected between B and C — its reading IS <var>V_{\mathrm{out}}</var>. The derivation below refers to these nodes by name.
ch1_4.loadingAria: Two side-by-side voltage-divider schematics. On the left, a divider with the output terminal open — the formula shows V-out equals V-in times R-two divided by R-one plus R-two. On the right, the same divider with a load resistor R-L connected across R-two; the output voltage is now lower and the formula now shows R-two in parallel with R-L instead of R-two alone.
ch1_4.intro: The resistor is the most common component in electronics — there are more of them on a typical circuit board than every other part combined. Chapter 1.2 treated the resistor as a pure value R; this chapter looks at the real thing on the bench: what it looks like, how to read its value off the coloured bands around its body, why you can buy 4.7 kΩ but almost never 5 kΩ, and how two or more of them combine when you wire them together. The last section introduces the single most useful two-resistor circuit in electronics — the voltage divider.
ch1_4.introPreview: We will meet the main resistor types you will encounter on a bench, decode the colour bands that spell out a resistor's value, see why the standard preferred-value series (E12, E24, E96…) exists and what it has to do with tolerance, combine resistors in series and in parallel with two simple formulas, and build a voltage divider that produces a smaller voltage from any fixed supply. Everything in this chapter leans on Ohm's law from Chapter 1.2 — re-read that section if the relationship between V, I, R, and P is still shaky.
ch1_4.sectionPackages: What a real resistor looks like
ch1_4.packagesIntro: Pick up any modern circuit board and the rectangular, sand-coloured chips scattered across it are almost all resistors. On a through-hole prototype — breadboard, veroboard, a kit you soldered yourself — they are small cylinders with a wire sticking out of each end and coloured bands around the body. Both are the same component: a slab or film of resistive material, trimmed to the target <var>R</var>, with leads or end-caps to connect it into the circuit.
ch1_4.packagesThroughHole: The through-hole axial resistor is the one with a body a few millimetres long and two long wires bent down through the board. It has been the hobby default for half a century. The resistive element is a spiral-cut film of metal, metal-oxide, or carbon deposited onto a ceramic rod; the spiral pitch sets the final value. The coloured bands are printed on the outside of the body once trimming is done. These are the resistors the colour code in the next section is built for.
ch1_4.packagesSMT: The surface-mount (SMT) chip resistor replaces the axial package in almost all mass-production today. It is a flat ceramic rectangle two millimetres long or less, with metallised ends that solder directly to pads on the board — no wires, no holes. The resistive film is screen-printed on the top surface. There are no coloured bands (the part is too small); instead, the value is printed as a 3- or 4-digit number. «472» means 47 × 10² = 4.7 kΩ; «1002» means 100 × 10² = 10 kΩ. Hobbyists sometimes solder «0805» chips by hand. Note that «0805» is not a value code — it is a separate size designator: imperial length × width in hundredths of an inch, so 0.08″ × 0.05″ = 2.0 × 1.3 mm. Other common sizes are «0603», «0402», «0201»; anything smaller than «0805» really wants a hot-air rework station.
ch1_4.packagesRange: Resistors are made in every value from about 0.2 Ω to 1 TΩ (a trillion ohms), with tolerances from ±20 % (bottom-of-the-barrel) down to ±0.005 % (instrumentation precision), and power ratings from ⅛ W up to 250 W for the big enamelled bricks in heater and dummy-load circuits. The three numbers — value, tolerance, power — are a package deal. You will see them together in every parts bin and every datasheet: «10 kΩ ±5 % ¼ W» means any actual resistance in the range 9.5 kΩ to 10.5 kΩ, safe to dissipate up to 0.25 W continuously.
ch1_4.packagesTypes: Underneath the package the resistive material matters too — briefly, for later chapters: metal-film (the modern default, ±1 % typical, low electrical noise — meaning the part itself generates only tiny random current fluctuations, which matters in amplifier front-ends), carbon-film (older, cheaper, ±5 %, fine for hobby work), wirewound (a real coil of resistance wire on a ceramic former — the big high-power bricks, also the most accurate precision parts, but behaves as an inductor at RF), and carbon-composition (a moulded slug of carbon-and-binder, now rare, historically the standard). For almost everything you build at this stage, metal-film or carbon-film at ±5 % is what you want.
ch1_4.colourCodeIntro: An axial resistor is too small to print numbers on, so the value is encoded as 3 to 6 coloured bands wrapping around the body. You read the bands starting from the end where they are grouped closest together, working toward the isolated tolerance band at the other end. Almost every hobby part is 4-band: two significant digits, one multiplier, one tolerance. Precision parts are 5-band: three significant digits, a multiplier, then tolerance.
ch1_4.colourCodeDigits: The code has a simple three-anchor shape — dark at the bottom, rainbow in the middle, light at the top: black (0) and brown (1) are the two dark anchors; red (2), orange (3), yellow (4), green (5), blue (6), violet (7) are the rainbow middle; grey (8) and white (9) are the two light anchors. Read it as a dark-to-light gradient and the mapping falls into place. The same colours serve as multipliers too — «brown» as a multiplier means ×10, «red» means ×100, and so on up through «white» (×10⁹) — with two extra entries: gold as a multiplier means ×0.1, silver means ×0.01 (for sub-ohm resistors). Tolerance bands use a shorter set: gold = ±5 %, silver = ±10 %, brown = ±1 %, red = ±2 %, and no band at all means ±20 %.
ch1_4.colourCodeWorkedExample: Worked example. A 4-band resistor with bands red, violet, orange, gold reads: red (2) — violet (7) — orange (×1000) — gold (±5 %). Significant digits give 27; multiply by 1000; the resistance is 27 kΩ at ±5 %. That means the actual value on a multimeter may be anywhere from 25.65 kΩ to 28.35 kΩ and still be within spec.
ch1_4.colourCodeDecoderIntro: Use the colour-chip rows below the resistor to set each band — every row corresponds to one band position. Reading order on a real resistor (and in the widget) is left-to-right: the first two (or three) bands are the digits, the next is the multiplier, the last is the tolerance. Toggle between 4- and 5-band mode to see how the precision grade works.
ch1_4.colourCodeNotation: You will also see a compact text notation in datasheets and schematics: 4k7 means 4.7 kΩ, 2R2 means 2.2 Ω, 1M0 means 1.0 MΩ. The letter takes the place of the decimal point AND names the multiplier at the same time, which turns out to be useful when you are photocopying schematics and the decimal points vanish into the toner.
ch1_4.colourCodeSMTNote: Surface-mount chips, as mentioned above, skip the colour code entirely. A 3-digit SMT code like 472 reads as «47 followed by 2 zeros» = 4700 Ω = 4.7 kΩ; 4-digit codes have three significant figures (4702 = 47.0 × 10² = 4.70 kΩ). A leading R acts like the letter in the «4k7» notation: R47 means 0.47 Ω, 4R7 means 4.7 Ω.
ch1_4.sectionESeries: Preferred values — why 4.7 kΩ and not 5 kΩ
ch1_4.eSeriesIntro: Open any parts catalogue and you will find 4.7 kΩ and 4.3 kΩ and 5.1 kΩ on the shelf, but not 4.5 kΩ or 5 kΩ. That is not laziness — it is a century-old standard called the <preferred>E-series</preferred> of preferred values, and it is tightly coupled to <tol>tolerance</tol>.
ch1_4.eSeriesWhy: The idea is this: each tolerance grade has a set of standard values spaced just wide enough that the ±window of one value meets the ±window of the next, with no gap and no overlap. If you manufacture a batch of ±5 % resistors and aim for 4.7 kΩ, the batch will be scattered between 4.47 kΩ and 4.94 kΩ. If the next standard value above is 5.1 kΩ (±5 % window 4.85 to 5.36 kΩ), there is a tiny overlap at the seam and the two bins together cover every value from 4.47 kΩ up to 5.36 kΩ. Pick finer tolerances and the bins need to be narrower — so there have to be more of them per decade.
ch1_4.eSeriesTable: The series is named after how many values sit in each decade (a factor of ten — 10 Ω, 100 Ω, 1 kΩ, 10 kΩ …). E12 (for ±10 % parts) has 12 values; E24 (±5 %) has 24; E48 (±2 %) has 48; E96 (±1 %) has 96; E192 (±0.5 % and tighter) has 192. Each decade repeats with a factor of 10: the E12 values are 10, 12, 15, 18, 22, 27, 33, 39, 47, 56, 68, 82 — so your parts bin has 10 Ω, 12 Ω, 15 Ω … 820 Ω, then 1.0 kΩ, 1.2 kΩ, 1.5 kΩ … and so on.
ch1_4.eSeriesSnapIntro: The widget below takes the value you wish you had and tells you the nearest real value you can actually buy in each E-series. Type «5000» — E12 lands on 4700 (no 5 kΩ available), E24 lands on 5100, E96 lands on 4990 (which is essentially 5 kΩ if you do not mind paying for ±1 %).
ch1_4.eSeriesCallout: Practical rule: design with an E24 value in mind. If the circuit really needs a value that is not in E24 — a precision divider, a matched pair — move up to E96 ±1 % for that one part; it is not expensive. Avoid designs that demand a value not in any standard series; the alternative is trimming two or more standard parts together into the right total, which is the next section.
ch1_4.sectionCombinations: Resistors in series and in parallel
ch1_4.combinationsIntro: Two resistors wired together act like a single equivalent resistor, and there are only two ways to wire two parts together: end-to-end (series) or side-by-side (parallel). Everything else you will ever see is built from these two primitives.
ch1_4.seriesFormula: Series — the two resistors share the same current, and the total voltage across both of them together equals the sum of the voltages across each one individually. The equivalent resistance is therefore the sum of the two values:
ch1_4.seriesIntuition: The series total is always larger than either of the two parts, because every electron still has to pass through both. Two 1 kΩ resistors in series give 2 kΩ; a 1 kΩ and a 10 kΩ give 11 kΩ.
ch1_4.parallelFormula: Parallel — the two resistors share the same voltage, and the currents through them add. The equivalent resistance equals the product of the two resistances divided by their sum:
ch1_4.parallelIntuition: The parallel total is always smaller than either of the two parts, because there are now two paths for current to split between. Two 1 kΩ resistors in parallel give 500 Ω. Two equal resistors in parallel always give half the value — and n equal resistors in parallel give R / n, a handy shortcut to keep in your head.
ch1_4.parallelDerivationSetup: Where that formula comes from. The product-over-sum formula looks opaque at first glance — it is not obvious where it comes from — but two physical facts force it. First, the two resistors both sit between the same pair of nodes — so they share the same voltage, call it V. Second, at the junction where they meet, the current coming in from the supply splits between them and nothing accumulates at the node; whatever current flows into a point in a wire must flow out again. (That rule is called Kirchhoff's current law; we will meet it properly later. For now it is just the obvious statement that charge does not accumulate at a point in a wire.)
ch1_4.parallelDerivationMathLead: The current through <var>R_{\mathrm{1}}</var> is <nowrap><var>V</var> / <var>R_{\mathrm{1}}</var></nowrap>; through <var>R_{\mathrm{2}}</var> is <nowrap><var>V</var> / <var>R_{\mathrm{2}}</var></nowrap>. Adding:
ch1_4.parallelDerivationReciprocalLead: Dividing both sides by V, the reciprocal of the combined resistance equals the sum of reciprocals — and this form generalises immediately to any number of parallel resistors:
ch1_4.parallelDerivationShortcutNote: The two-resistor product-over-sum form from the previous paragraph is just this equation cleared of fractions for <var>n</var> = 2 — an algebraic shortcut, not a deeper truth. For three or more resistors in parallel, use the reciprocal form directly; there is no equivalently clean product-over-sum shortcut.
ch1_4.parallelDerivationConductance: The reciprocal form also reveals a neat symmetry. Rename 1 / R as a quantity in its own right — conductance, symbol G, measured in siemens (S). A 1 Ω resistor has a conductance of 1 S; a 1 kΩ resistor, 1 mS; a 1 MΩ resistor, 1 µS. Large resistance ↔ small conductance, and vice versa.
ch1_4.parallelDerivationDualityLead: With conductance as the variable, the parallel rule becomes a simple sum — structurally identical to the series rule for resistance:
ch1_4.parallelDerivationClose: Series adds resistance; parallel adds conductance. That duality — two views of the same circuit arithmetic — is a recurring feature of linear circuits. Hence a useful habit worth internalising now: pick the variable that makes the sum easier. We will meet conductance again in the chapter on filters (1.8), where the input conductance of the next stage tells you how hard it is loading the previous one.
ch1_4.combinationsCalcIntro: The calculator below runs both formulas at once on whatever pair of values you enter, so you can see the series sum and the parallel product shoulder-to-shoulder. Try a pair where one value is far bigger than the other — a 1 kΩ and a 1 MΩ, say — and notice how lopsided the two results are.
ch1_4.shortcutCallout: Keep-in-your-head shortcut. When one resistor is much larger than the other (say 10× or more), the dominant value is the one that matters: in series, the larger resistor dominates (1 kΩ + 1 MΩ ≈ 1 MΩ), and in parallel, the smaller resistor dominates (1 kΩ 1 MΩ ≈ 1 kΩ). That lets you sanity-check a combination at a glance, before reaching for the calculator.
ch1_4.combinationsTrimming: You can use this to «trim» a standard value up or down: start with an E24 resistor near the target, then add a much smaller one in series (to nudge the total up by a percent or so) or a much larger one in parallel (to nudge down). Two ±5 % parts in series give a total whose tolerance is still roughly ±5 %, but you can hit values that are not on any E-series shelf.
ch1_4.dividerIntro: The single most useful two-resistor circuit is the <divider>voltage divider</divider>: two resistors wired in series across a supply; its output is the voltage at the node where the two resistors meet. This one circuit, with no active components, shows up in almost every sensor, every biasing network, and every input stage of a piece of test equipment that needs to scale the input signal down before measuring it.
ch1_4.dividerFormulaPoints: Look at the schematic. Three labelled nodes: A on the top rail (connected directly to V_{\mathrm{in}}), B at the junction between R_{\mathrm{1}} and R_{\mathrm{2}} (the point V_{\mathrm{out}} is tapped from), and C on the bottom rail (the ground reference, 0 V). The full input voltage V_{\mathrm{in}} sits between A and C; the output we are looking for, V_{\mathrm{out}}, is the voltage between B and C.
ch1_4.dividerFormulaCurrentLead: Step 1 — find the current. R_{\mathrm{1}} and R_{\mathrm{2}} are in series, so the same current flows through both. The total resistance seen by V_{\mathrm{in}} between A and C is R_{\mathrm{1}} + R_{\mathrm{2}}, so by Ohm's law the current is:
ch1_4.dividerFormulaOutputLead: Step 2 — find the voltage across R_{\mathrm{2}}. That same current flows through R_{\mathrm{2}}, and the voltage across it is, again by Ohm's law, the current times R_{\mathrm{2}}. That voltage — between B and C — is exactly what the voltmeter on the schematic reads, and it is V_{\mathrm{out}}:
ch1_4.dividerFormulaSubstLead: Step 3 — substitute. Replace the I from step 2 with its expression from step 1, and V_{\mathrm{out}} comes out entirely in terms of what we know — the input voltage and the two resistances:
ch1_4.dividerFormulaNote: That fraction, <nowrap><var>R_{\mathrm{2}}</var> / (<var>R_{\mathrm{1}}</var> + <var>R_{\mathrm{2}}</var>)</nowrap>, is the divide ratio. It is between 0 and 1, and it depends only on the ratio of the two resistors, not on their absolute values. A 10 kΩ–10 kΩ divider gives exactly half the input; a 1 kΩ–1 kΩ divider also gives exactly half. The absolute values set the current the divider draws from the supply and the power each resistor has to dissipate — about which, in a moment.
ch1_4.dividerWidgetIntro: Drag the three sliders below and watch <var>V_{\mathrm{out}}</var> track. Two observations as you play: the output is always less than the input (hence «divider», never «multiplier»), and doubling <var>R_{\mathrm{1}}</var> and <var>R_{\mathrm{2}}</var> together leaves <var>V_{\mathrm{out}}</var> unchanged but halves the current drawn from the supply.
ch1_4.loadingIntro: The formula above assumes nothing is connected to the output — the divider is floating. Connect a load (the next stage of the circuit, a microcontroller input, another resistor) across <var>R_{\mathrm{2}}</var>, and the load pulls extra current through <var>R_{\mathrm{1}}</var>. That extra current means extra voltage drop across <var>R_{\mathrm{1}}</var>, and the output falls below the formula's prediction. The heavier the load (the lower its resistance), the bigger the sag.
ch1_4.loadingRule: Rule of thumb: to keep the output within about 10 % of its no-load value, pick the load resistance R_{\mathrm{L}} at least 10 times R_{\mathrm{2}} (the lower resistor of the divider). Then the parallel combination R_{\mathrm{2}} R_{\mathrm{L}} — the very one you see in the loaded-divider formula on the diagram above — is nearly equal to R_{\mathrm{2}} itself, and the divide ratio barely changes. For a 10 kΩ–10 kΩ divider that means a 100 kΩ or heavier load.
ch1_4.loadingBridge: A tighter analysis of the sag, and its connection to Thévenin's theorem, comes in Chapter 1.7. For now the takeaway is just this: a divider is not a battery. It is a good, cheap, boring way to produce a smaller voltage, but only as long as whatever drinks from it draws very little current.
ch1_4.sectionPower: Power rating — why the resistor in your divider might smoke
ch1_4.powerIntro: Every resistor in a working circuit is converting electrical power to heat at a rate <nowrap><var>P</var> = <var>I</var>² · <var>R</var> = <var>V</var>² / <var>R</var></nowrap> (Chapter 1.2). Push that rate past the <rating>power rating</rating> stamped on the part's datasheet and the resistor overheats — first it drifts in value, then it discolours, then it burns open.
ch1_4.powerDividerExample: Take a 12 V supply across a 100 Ω–100 Ω divider. Current: <nowrap>12 V / 200 Ω = 60 mA</nowrap>. Power in each 100 Ω resistor: <nowrap>(0.06)² × 100 = 0.36 W</nowrap>. A ¼ W (0.25 W) resistor in that divider is overloaded by 44 % — it will run hot, drift, and eventually fail. A ½ W resistor would be fine but marginal; a 1 W resistor gives proper margin.
ch1_4.powerAoEExercise: Useful exercise: with a 15 V supply, what is the smallest resistor value you can safely use as a single series element on a ¼ W resistor, regardless of what else is in the circuit? The worst case is that the resistor sees the full 15 V across itself, so P = V² / R = 225 / R; to keep that at or below 0.25 W, R must be at least 900 Ω. Round up to the nearest E-series value — 910 Ω (E24) or 1 kΩ (both E12 and E24) — and you are safe for any configuration.
ch1_4.powerDeratingCallout: Design rule: pick a resistor rated at 2 to 4 times the worst-case dissipation. A resistor running right at its rating is one sunny day in a closed enclosure away from failing; at 25–50 % of rating it runs cool and lasts.

colour.title: Resistor colour-code decoder
colour.description: Pick a colour in each row to set the corresponding band. Value, tolerance, and text-notation equivalent update live.
colour.hint: Tip: read a real resistor from the end where the bands are grouped closest — that is the digit end. The lone band near the other end is the tolerance.

eSeries.title: Snap to a preferred value
eSeries.description: Type any resistance you would like. The widget finds the nearest standard value in each E-series so you know what to actually buy.
eSeries.hint: The «Error» column shows how far the nearest preferred value is from your target, as a percentage. Tighter tolerance series give smaller errors — E24 ±5 % covers most hobby builds, while precision dividers or matched pairs justify E96.

combine.title: Series and parallel calculator
combine.description: Two resistors at once. Left column is the series total, right is the parallel equivalent — compare them on every combination you try.
combine.hint: Push one value ten times larger than the other and watch the asymmetry: series ≈ the bigger one, parallel ≈ the smaller one.

divider.title: Voltage divider
divider.description: Pick the input voltage and the two resistors; the widget shows the no-load output, the current drawn from the supply, and (with the load switched in) how much the output sags.
divider.hint: The rule-of-thumb says keep <var>R_{\mathrm{load}}</var> ≥ 10 · R₂ (the lower divider resistor). Turn the load on and push <var>R_{\mathrm{load}}</var> below that threshold — watch <var>V_{\mathrm{out}}</var> collapse.

ch1_4.keyTakeaway1: A real resistor has three numbers attached: value (ohms), tolerance (percent), and power rating (watts). All three matter.
ch1_4.keyTakeaway2: The colour code reads digit–digit–(digit–)multiplier–tolerance from the closest-grouped end. 4-band is hobby standard; 5-band is precision. «4k7» notation means 4.7 kΩ.
ch1_4.keyTakeaway3: Preferred values (E12, E24, E96 …) are logarithmically spaced so that adjacent tolerance windows meet. Design for E24 by default and step up to E96 when you need precision.
ch1_4.keyTakeaway4: Series: <nowrap><var>R</var> = <var>R_{\mathrm{1}}</var> + <var>R_{\mathrm{2}}</var></nowrap> — total is larger than either resistor on its own. Parallel: <nowrap><var>R</var> = <var>R_{\mathrm{1}}</var>·<var>R_{\mathrm{2}}</var> / (<var>R_{\mathrm{1}}</var> + <var>R_{\mathrm{2}}</var>)</nowrap> — total is smaller than either. Mismatched values: series dominated by the bigger, parallel by the smaller.
ch1_4.keyTakeaway5: Voltage divider: <nowrap><var>V_{\mathrm{out}}</var> = <var>V_{\mathrm{in}}</var> · <var>R_{\mathrm{2}}</var> / (<var>R_{\mathrm{1}}</var> + <var>R_{\mathrm{2}}</var>)</nowrap>. Output sags under load; keep load resistance at least ten times <var>R_{\mathrm{2}}</var> (the lower resistor of the divider).
ch1_4.labGoal: Decode five resistors by their colour bands, verify each one with a multimeter, build a voltage divider on a breadboard, and measure how the output sags when a load is connected.
ch1_4.labEquip1: A handful of through-hole resistors of various values — at least five different ones, any mix of E12/E24 values from ~100 Ω to ~100 kΩ.
ch1_4.labEquip2: <multimeter>Multimeter</multimeter> with a resistance (Ω) range and a <dc>DC</dc>-volts range.
ch1_4.labEquip3: A <breadboard>breadboard</breadboard> and a pair of 1.5 V AA cells (in a two-cell holder, giving 3 V).
ch1_4.labEquip4: A pencil and a notebook — you will record the printed value, the colour bands, the measured value, and the percent error for each resistor.
ch1_4.labComp1: Five mixed resistors (the ones you will identify).
ch1_4.labComp2: Two chosen resistors for the divider — suggest one around 10 kΩ and one around 4.7 kΩ.
ch1_4.labComp3: One extra resistor to use as the load — start with 100 kΩ, then try 10 kΩ, then 1 kΩ.
ch1_4.labStep1: For each of the five mystery resistors, before measuring: write down the four colour bands in order and decode the value using the tables in this chapter. Write the predicted value and the ±tolerance percentage in your notebook.
ch1_4.labStep2: Set the multimeter to its resistance range (Ω, kΩ or autorange). Touch the probes together first — the display should read close to 0 Ω; if it reads 0.3 Ω or so, that is your probe-lead resistance and you can subtract it mentally from later readings. Measure each resistor by touching one probe to each lead. Record the measured value next to your predicted value.
ch1_4.labStep3: Compute the percent error for each resistor: <nowrap>100 · (measured − nominal) / nominal</nowrap>. Check that every resistor's measured value falls inside its tolerance band. A ±5 % part (gold tolerance band) should measure within ±5 %; if one is outside that window, the part is bad — or more likely, you mis-read the bands, so redo step 1.
ch1_4.labStep4: Now build the divider. Put the two AA cells in the holder (3.0 V nominal, real value a bit below). Connect the holder's + lead to one end of the 10 kΩ resistor; the other end of the 10 kΩ to one end of the 4.7 kΩ; the other end of the 4.7 kΩ back to the holder's − lead. The junction between the two resistors is <var>V_{\mathrm{out}}</var>.
ch1_4.labStep5: Measure the DC voltage from V_{\mathrm{out}} to the − lead of the cells. Predict first. Using V_{\mathrm{out}} = V_{\mathrm{in}} · 4.7 / (10 + 4.7) and the actual battery voltage you measure across the holder, compute the expected reading. Then measure. They should agree to better than a couple of percent — the multimeter's input impedance (10 MΩ) is a very light load on a divider whose parallel resistance is only about 3.2 kΩ.
ch1_4.labStep6: Connect the 100 kΩ load resistor from <var>V_{\mathrm{out}}</var> to the − lead. Re-measure <var>V_{\mathrm{out}}</var>. It should sag by a small but visible amount (roughly 3 %). Replace the 100 kΩ with the 10 kΩ; re-measure. Now the sag is large — the rule of thumb «keep the load at least ten times the divider's parallel resistance» is being violated. Replace with the 1 kΩ load and see the divider collapse.
ch1_4.labExpected: Decoded values match measured values to within each resistor's tolerance band. The divider outputs ≈ 32 % of the supply (4.7 / (10 + 4.7)) when floating; sags by a few percent under a 100 kΩ load, noticeably under a 10 kΩ load, and severely under a 1 kΩ load.
ch1_4.labConnection: This lab links the static facts of the colour code to the behaviour of resistors inside a working circuit. The divider you built is one of the most common circuits in electronics — the same topology drives sensor readouts, microcontroller reference voltages, and the first stage of oscilloscope probes. The loading experiment is your first encounter with the fact that circuits interact when you connect them, which motivates the Thévenin-equivalent discussion in Chapter 1.7.
ch1_4.labTrouble1: The multimeter reads wildly different values depending on how hard you press. You have an oxidation problem on the resistor leads — wipe them with fine sandpaper or a pencil eraser before inserting into the breadboard. Breadboard contacts also oxidise over time; move to a fresh row.
ch1_4.labTrouble2: The divider output is zero, not a fraction of the input. Almost always a wiring error: one of the two resistors is not actually in circuit (bent lead, bad contact). Remove the resistors, measure each one in isolation, then rebuild the divider carefully.
ch1_4.labTrouble3: The measured divider voltage disagrees with prediction by far more than a few percent. Measure the cell voltage first — tired AAs can sit well below 1.5 V each — and use that actual V_{\mathrm{in}} in your prediction. If the disagreement persists, measure both resistors in isolation; you may have picked up a wrong value.
ch1_4.quiz_q1: A 4-band resistor has bands red, violet, orange, gold. What is its value and tolerance?
ch1_4.quiz_q1_explanation: Red = 2, violet = 7, orange = ×1000, gold = ±5 %. 27 × 1000 = 27 kΩ at ±5 %.
ch1_4.quiz_q2: Two 10 kΩ resistors are connected in parallel. What is the equivalent resistance?
ch1_4.quiz_q2_explanation: Two equal resistors in parallel give half their value: 10 kΩ / 2 = 5 kΩ.
ch1_4.quiz_q3: A 1 kΩ resistor is in parallel with a 100 kΩ resistor. Roughly, what is the combined resistance?
ch1_4.quiz_q3_explanation: In parallel the smaller resistor dominates. Exact value: 1·100 / 101 ≈ 0.990 kΩ — slightly under 1 kΩ.
ch1_4.quiz_q4: A 12 V supply feeds a voltage divider with <nowrap><var>R_{\mathrm{1}}</var> = 10 kΩ</nowrap> on top and <nowrap><var>R_{\mathrm{2}}</var> = 5 kΩ</nowrap> on the bottom. What is <var>V_{\mathrm{out}}</var> (no load)?
ch1_4.quiz_q5: A 100 Ω resistor has 10 V across it. How much power does it dissipate?
ch1_4.quiz_q5_explanation: <nowrap><var>P</var> = <var>V</var>² / <var>R</var> = 100 / 100 = 1 W</nowrap>. A ¼ W part here would burn; you want at least a 2 W rating.
ch1_4.quiz_q6: You see «4k7» printed on a schematic next to a resistor. What value is that?
ch1_4.quiz_q6_explanation: The letter k marks the decimal point AND the kilo multiplier. 4k7 = 4.7 kΩ. Similarly 2R2 = 2.2 Ω and 1M0 = 1.0 MΩ.
ch1_4.quiz_q7: You load a 10 kΩ–10 kΩ divider with a 5 kΩ load across R₂. Will the output sag noticeably?
ch1_4.quiz_q7_b: Yes — 5 kΩ equals the divider's 5 kΩ output resistance, so the output drops to about half of its no-load value.
ch1_4.quiz_q7_c: No, dividers do not sag under load.
ch1_4.quiz_q7_d: Yes, but only by a fraction of a percent.
ch1_4.quiz_q7_explanation: The rule-of-thumb wants <nowrap><var>R_{\mathrm{L}}</var> ≥ 10 · R₂ = 100 kΩ</nowrap>. Here <var>R_{\mathrm{L}}</var> = 5 kΩ — 20 times smaller than recommended, so the sag is significant. Actual calculation: <nowrap>R₂ <pll /> <var>R_{\mathrm{L}}</var> = 10 kΩ <pll /> 5 kΩ ≈ 3.33 kΩ</nowrap>, so <nowrap><var>V_{\mathrm{out}}</var> = <var>V_{\mathrm{in}}</var> · 3.33 / (10 + 3.33) ≈ 0.25 · <var>V_{\mathrm{in}}</var></nowrap>.
