That is half of the no-load 0.5 · <var>V_{\mathrm{in}}</var>.
ch1_4.quiz_q8: Why is 5 kΩ effectively unavailable as an off-the-shelf through-hole resistor, while 4.7 kΩ and 5.1 kΩ are both common?
ch1_4.quiz_q8_a: Historical accident — there is no real reason.
ch1_4.quiz_q8_b: 5 kΩ is too hard to manufacture accurately.
ch1_4.quiz_q8_c: 4.7 kΩ and 5.1 kΩ are adjacent values in the E24 preferred-value series; 5 kΩ is not in any standard E-series below E192.
ch1_4.quiz_q8_explanation: E24 (±5 %) goes 4.3, 4.7, 5.1, 5.6 kΩ … with gaps sized to match the ±5 % tolerance window. 5 kΩ falls between 4.7 and 5.1 — both values cover it within their tolerances, so the series does not need a dedicated 5 kΩ entry.

ch1_5.heroAriaLabel: A hand-drawn cross-section of a parallel-plate capacitor: two horizontal plates marked plus and minus, separated by a shaded dielectric layer between them, with electric-field arrows pointing from the positive plate to the negative one. Leads extend from each plate outward, labelled positive and negative, so the reader can see at a glance that charge sits on the plates and the electric field lives in the dielectric.
ch1_5.intro: A capacitor is a device that stores energy in an electric field between two conductors. In Chapter 1.4 you treated the resistor as a pure value R — one number, no memory, voltage and current in step. A capacitor has memory: it charges up when you push current into it, holds that charge when you stop, and discharges it back when you let it. Three one-sentence consequences carry almost every application you will meet in radio: it blocks DC (no steady current can flow through it once it is charged), it passes AC (alternating current sloshes the stored charge back and forth), and it integrates current over time (the longer you pump current in, the higher the voltage climbs).
ch1_5.introPreview: We will start with the defining equation Q = CV and the farad, build physical intuition with a widget that lets you push plates apart or swap dielectrics, tour the three capacitor types you will actually meet on the shelf (ceramic, film, electrolytic), combine capacitors in series and parallel (with a twist — the formulas are the opposite of resistors), meet the RC time constant and the 5τ rule, and close with an intuitive look at blocks-DC-passes-AC that sets up the reactance discussion in Chapter 1.7.
ch1_5.whatItIsIntro: Stand two metal plates facing each other with an insulator between them and connect a battery across them. The battery pushes electrons onto one plate and pulls them off the other until the voltage between the plates matches its own. Then current stops — the insulator does not conduct — but the plates are now storing an imbalance of charge. Disconnect the battery and the charge stays put; short the plates together and it flows back. The device stores energy, and the quantity that tells you how much charge it holds per volt applied is its <cap>capacitance</cap>, symbol <var>C</var>.
ch1_5.whatItIsQV: The defining equation is disarmingly simple — the charge on the plates is proportional to the voltage across them, and the constant of proportionality is the capacitance:
ch1_5.whatItIsDefinitions: Charge Q is measured in coulombs (C) — the same unit from Chapter 1.1. Capacitance C is measured in farads (F). One farad is the capacitance that holds one coulomb of charge for every volt applied. A farad is an enormous amount of capacitance — almost every capacitor you will ever wire up is specified in microfarads (µF = 10⁻⁶ F), nanofarads (nF = 10⁻⁹ F), or picofarads (pF = 10⁻¹² F). A 1 F «supercapacitor» is the size of a large coin; a 1 pF ceramic chip is smaller than a pinhead.
ch1_5.whatItIsRateForm: Take the <deriv>derivative</deriv> of <nowrap><var>Q</var> = <var>C</var><var>V</var></nowrap> with respect to time — capacitance is fixed, so — and you get the working form, which is how engineers actually think about capacitors in circuits:
ch1_5.whatItIsRateNote: Current equals capacitance times the rate of change of voltage. Read it both ways. If the voltage across a capacitor is held steady, dV/dt = 0 and the current is zero — that is «blocks DC», one equation deep. If the voltage changes, current flows; faster changes demand more current — that is «passes AC». Worked number: push a constant 1 mA into a 1 µF capacitor and its voltage rises at 1000 V/s; a 10 ms burst of that current raises it by 10 V. This is exactly how every capacitor-based timer works.
ch1_5.sectionGeometry: What sets the value — plates, spacing, dielectric
ch1_5.geometryIntro: The capacitance of an idealised pair of parallel plates depends on three things — and only three things:
ch1_5.geometryVars: A is the area of overlap between the plates (larger area ↔ more room to store charge); d is the distance between them (smaller spacing ↔ stronger electric field per volt ↔ more charge stored); ε_{\mathrm{r}} is the relative permittivity, or dielectric constant, of the insulator between the plates — a pure number that tells you how much better the material stores a field than a vacuum. ε_{\mathrm{0}} is the permittivity of free space, a physical constant ≈ 8.85 pF/m.
ch1_5.geometryDielectrics: A vacuum is the reference — ε_{\mathrm{r}} = 1 by definition. Air is essentially the same (1.0006). Common insulators do better: polyester ≈ 3, ceramic C0G ≈ 45, X7R ceramic ≈ 3000, aluminium oxide ≈ 8, tantalum pentoxide ≈ 28. The exotic value isn't just academic — a 10 µF ceramic capacitor is physically smaller than a 10 µF air-gap capacitor would be, by the ratio of their dielectric constants.
ch1_5.geometryBuilderIntro: The widget below lets you set <var>A</var>, <var>d</var>, and pick a dielectric from the menu; it computes the capacitance and autoscales the unit (pF / nF / µF). Try to build 1 µF from air-gap plates — even with dinner-table-sized plates you will need to squeeze the gap down to a fraction of a human hair.
ch1_5.sectionTypes: Types you will meet on the shelf
ch1_5.typesIntro: Capacitors come in more flavours than any other passive component — every family exploits a different compromise between capacitance density, precision, leakage, frequency response, size, and cost. Three families cover almost everything you will wire up at this stage; the others are briefly previewed so you recognise them in datasheets.
ch1_5.typesCeramicIntro: Ceramic capacitors use a ceramic wafer as the dielectric, with metal electrodes screen-printed on top. The two sub-species matter: Class 1 (C0G or NP0) uses a low-permittivity, temperature-stable ceramic — modest capacitance (picofarads to a few nanofarads), nearly perfect stability with temperature, voltage, and age, and negligible loss. These are the capacitors you want in RF oscillators and filters. Class 2 (X7R, Y5V) uses a high-permittivity, unstable ceramic that lets one chip pack microfarads into a millimetre-sized package — cheap, compact, ideal for bypass and decoupling. But you pay for the density: capacitance drops 20 % with temperature and, more dramatically, with applied DC voltage. A 10 µF X7R rated 25 V can measure 3 µF when biased at 20 V.
ch1_5.typesCeramicCallout: DC-bias gotcha (X7R). Class-2 ceramic capacitors lose a surprising fraction of their capacitance when you apply DC voltage near their rating — commonly 50 % at half-rated voltage, 70–80 % near the rating. The number stamped on the part is measured at 0 V DC. For decoupling this is usually fine (you want «at least 100 nF», not exactly 100 nF). For a timing circuit or a tuned filter, never use X7R — pick C0G, film, or electrolytic depending on the value.
ch1_5.typesFilmIntro: Film capacitors use a thin polymer film (polyester / Mylar, polypropylene, polycarbonate) metallised on one side, then rolled or stacked. Values run from hundreds of pF up to a few µF; tolerance is tight (±5 % or better); temperature stability is excellent; there is no DC-bias drift. Film caps are the goldilocks choice for audio coupling, timing circuits, and anywhere you need a precise, stable value above what C0G ceramic covers. The trade-off is physical size — a 1 µF film cap is a finger-sized brick, while a 1 µF X7R is an 0805 chip smaller than a grain of rice.
ch1_5.typesElectrolyticIntro: Electrolytic capacitors use a very thin oxide layer grown on a metal foil as the dielectric, with the second electrode being a conductive electrolyte (liquid or gel). The thinness of the oxide and the effective area of porous foils gives enormous capacitance — 10 µF to several thousand µF at everyday voltage ratings — in a can the size of a pea or a thumb. Two species: aluminium electrolytics are the cheap workhorses for power-supply smoothing and bulk storage; tantalum electrolytics are smaller and have lower equivalent series resistance (ESR) but are brittle — exceed their rating or reverse their polarity even briefly and they can fail short, which in a dense board can literally catch fire.
ch1_5.typesElectrolyticCallout: Polarity — absolutely non-negotiable. The oxide dielectric is grown electrochemically in ONE direction. Reverse voltage breaks it down: an aluminium electrolytic heats, bulges, vents its electrolyte, and stops working; a tantalum can explode. The longer lead (or the un-striped lead on SMT tantalums) is positive; the stripe on an aluminium can marks the negative lead. If the markings are rubbed off or the cap is a salvage part, the honest move is to grab a fresh one. If you do need to check with a multimeter: in capacitance mode (—||— symbol) the correct polarity reads a stable value close to the rated capacitance, and the red probe is on the «+» lead; reversed, the meter shows «OL» or drifts. For aluminium you can also use diode-test mode briefly, but never on tantalum — even a second of reverse voltage in diode mode damages the oxide.
ch1_5.typesOthers: Briefly, so you recognise them: silver-mica (very stable, 1 pF to 10 nF, classic in vintage RF oscillators); paper / oil-filled (high-voltage-tolerant legacy parts, replaced by film in new designs); supercapacitors (1 F and up at a few volts — for backup power and energy harvesting, not signal-path work); vacuum capacitors (tens to hundreds of pF at tens of kilovolts — exclusively for transmitter output networks). You will meet most of these again in later chapters when the context calls for them.
ch1_5.typesGalleryAria: A hand-drawn row of six labelled capacitor silhouettes side by side: a small flat disc with two wire leads ("disc ceramic"), a stack of rectangles with two wire leads ("MLCC"), a rectangular cylinder with two axial leads ("film"), a tall can with two downward radial leads ("radial electrolytic"), a sideways cylinder with two axial leads and a plus-sign on one end ("axial electrolytic"), and a small teardrop shape with two short leads and a stripe ("tantalum"). Each silhouette is labelled underneath.
ch1_5.combinationsIntro: Wire two capacitors together and the same two rules apply — they either end up side-by-side (parallel) or end-to-end (series). The formulas are, surprisingly, the opposite of resistors: capacitors add in parallel and combine reciprocally in series. Once you see why, it is obvious; but it is the most commonly confused pair in beginner electronics, so take a minute on it.
ch1_5.parallelFormulaLead: Parallel. Two capacitors between the same pair of nodes share the same voltage. Since Q = CV, the charges on the two caps add up — the pair stores Q_{\mathrm{1}} + Q_{\mathrm{2}} = (C_{\mathrm{1}} + C_{\mathrm{2}}) · V coulombs at the same voltage. That is exactly the same response as a single capacitor of value C_{\mathrm{1}} + C_{\mathrm{2}}:
ch1_5.parallelIntuition: The parallel total is always larger than either part — you are literally adding plate area. Two 10 µF caps in parallel give 20 µF.
ch1_5.seriesFormulaLead: Series. In a series pair, only the two outer plates connect to the battery; the two inner plates connect to each other through a single wire — and to nothing else. That middle wire and the two plates it joins form an electrically isolated island: no third wire feeds charge in or out, so the island's total charge cannot change from what it was before power was applied: zero. When the battery pushes +Q onto the left outer plate, that charge attracts −Q onto the first inner plate. For the island to stay neutral overall, the second inner plate must then carry +Q — charge has simply shifted across the connecting wire. That +Q, in turn, attracts −Q onto the right outer plate, closing the loop back to the battery. Every one of the four plates ends up carrying the same magnitude of charge Q — this is what «series capacitors share the same charge» really means. The voltage across each is V_{i} = Q / C_{i}; the total voltage is the sum:
ch1_5.seriesFormulaReciprocal: Reading that as <nowrap><var>V</var> = <var>Q</var> · (…)</nowrap>, the equivalent capacitance — the one that would give the same <nowrap><var>V</var> / <var>Q</var></nowrap> ratio — has to satisfy <nowrap>1 / <var>C</var> = 1 / <var>C_{\mathrm{1}}</var> + 1 / <var>C_{\mathrm{2}}</var></nowrap>:
ch1_5.seriesShortcut: For two capacitors only, that reciprocal form collapses to a product-over-sum:
ch1_5.seriesIntuition: The series total is always smaller than either part — you are effectively increasing the dielectric thickness (thinking of the two caps as one cap with double the plate spacing). Two 10 µF caps in series give 5 µF.
ch1_5.seriesVoltageSharing: Voltage-sharing twist — and a safety issue. In a resistor series chain, a bigger resistor sees more voltage. In a capacitor series chain it is the opposite: the smaller capacitor sees the larger voltage, because the charge is shared but the voltage-per-coulomb is 1/C. For mismatched capacitors this matters: two caps in series across a 400 V supply, one 1 µF and one 0.1 µF, see 36 V and 364 V respectively — the 0.1 µF has to withstand almost the full supply on its own. In high-voltage power-supply designs, engineers place equalising resistors in parallel with each series capacitor to keep the voltages balanced.
ch1_5.combinationsSchematicAria: Two side-by-side capacitor schematics. On the left, two capacitors C-one and C-two drawn in parallel between the same pair of terminals, with a formula C equals C-one plus C-two underneath. On the right, the same two capacitors drawn in series between two terminals with a formula C equals C-one times C-two divided by C-one plus C-two underneath.
ch1_5.combinationsCalcIntro: The calculator below runs both formulas on whatever pair of values you enter. Notice that mismatched values are dominated by the larger cap in parallel and by the smaller cap in series — the exact mirror of the resistor rule.
ch1_5.energyIntro: When you charge a capacitor through any path — even a perfect zero-resistance one — you do work against the rising plate voltage. That work is not lost as heat (a capacitor doesn't dissipate anything); it is stored in the electric field between the plates. The amount, derived by integrating V · dQ from zero to the final charge, is:
ch1_5.energyWorkedExample: A 470 µF capacitor charged to 12 V stores <nowrap><var>U</var> = 0.5 · 470·10⁻⁶ · 12² = 0.034 J</nowrap> — modest. But a 4700 µF supply-reservoir capacitor at 400 V stores <nowrap>0.5 · 4700·10⁻⁶ · 400² = 376 J</nowrap>. That is enough to weld a screwdriver across the terminals, which is why tube-amplifier power supplies have big warnings and slow bleeder resistors. Always verify with a voltmeter before touching the terminals of a large capacitor.
ch1_5.rcIntro: Wire a resistor R in series with a capacitor C between the positive and negative terminals of a supply V_{\mathrm{in}}. Close the switch. In the very first instant the cap is uncharged (V_{\mathrm{C}} = 0) — at that moment it behaves exactly like a plain wire, offering no opposition to the current. All of V_{\mathrm{in}} falls across R, and the current hits its maximum value V_{\mathrm{in}} / R. That is precisely why the resistor is there: without it, nothing would limit this first-moment current — it would be driven to infinity by an ideal supply against an ideal wire. As charge accumulates on the plates, V_{\mathrm{C}} rises, the voltage across the resistor (which is what drives the current) shrinks to V_{\mathrm{in}} − V_{\mathrm{C}}, the current tapers, and V_{\mathrm{C}} creeps ever more slowly toward V_{\mathrm{in}} but never quite reaches it (the mathematician’s word for this is «asymptotic»). The waveform is an exponential:
ch1_5.rcTauDefn: The product R · C has units of seconds (Ω · F = s — you can verify that by chasing SI units through Ohm's law and Q = CV) and is called the time constant, symbol τ (Greek tau). It sets the whole timescale of the charge curve. Two handy shortcuts to memorise:
ch1_5.rcShortcuts: MΩ × µF = seconds. kΩ × µF = milliseconds. A 10 kΩ resistor charging a 10 µF cap has τ = 100 ms. A 1 MΩ resistor charging a 1 nF cap has τ = 1 ms.
ch1_5.rcCurvePoints: At t = τ, the capacitor has reached 63.2 % of V_{\mathrm{in}}. At 2τ, 86.5 %. At 3τ, 95.0 %. At 4τ, 98.2 %. At 5τ, 99.3 % — the «five time constants» rule of thumb that engineers use to declare a capacitor «charged» (or «discharged», for the mirror-image decay curve). For any design purpose — debouncing a switch, smoothing a supply, blocking DC in an audio coupler — 5τ is the right number to reach for.
ch1_5.rcDischargeDerivation: Discharge is symmetric. Replace the supply with a short and the same capacitor now pushes current backward through R; the voltage decays from its initial value V_{\mathrm{0}} along the same time constant:
ch1_5.rcWidgetIntro: The widget below lets you pick <var>R</var> and <var>C</var>, hit «charge» or «discharge», and watch the exponential trace with gridlines at each τ. Notice that after 5τ the capacitor is essentially done, and that doubling either <var>R</var> or <var>C</var> doubles τ.
ch1_5.rcSafetyCallout: Never trust a bleeder alone. High-voltage power supplies typically have a «bleeder resistor» wired across the output capacitor to drain it when powered off. A 100 µF cap shunted by a 100 kΩ bleeder has τ = 10 s, so 5τ = 50 s — which sounds safe, but bleeders fail open, and a failed bleeder on a 400 V supply is an electrocution hazard that you cannot see just by looking at the board. Before touching anything, measure the capacitor voltage with a meter.
ch1_5.sectionBlocksDcPassesAc: «Blocks DC, passes AC» — a first look
ch1_5.blocksIntro: The phrase is in every capacitor datasheet and every introductory text for a reason — it is the single most important behaviour in practice, and it follows from one equation you already have. I = C · dV/dt: the current through a capacitor is the capacitance times the rate of change of voltage across it. Put a pure DC voltage across a cap and, once it has charged up, the voltage stops changing — dV/dt = 0 — so no current flows. The cap is acting as an open circuit to DC.
ch1_5.blocksPassesAc: Put an AC voltage across the same cap and dV/dt is nonzero throughout every cycle — current flows. The higher the frequency, the faster the voltage changes, the larger the current — so the cap looks more and more like a short circuit as frequency rises. The quantitative story is capacitive reactance, X_{\mathrm{C}} = 1 / (2πfC): an impedance in ohms that depends on both the capacitance and the frequency. At 1 kHz a 1 µF cap looks like ≈ 160 Ω; at 1 MHz the same cap looks like 0.16 Ω. We save the full reactance treatment for Chapter 1.7 — where, together with inductors, it becomes the core of filters, tuned circuits, and resonance.
ch1_5.blocksApplications: Two applications of «blocks DC, passes AC» dominate practical circuits. You will see both at every scale of electronics work.
ch1_5.blocksCouplingCap: Coupling (in series with the signal path). Put a capacitor in series between two stages whose DC levels differ — say a microphone sitting at 0 V feeding an amplifier whose input is biased at +2 V. The cap blocks the DC offset (the amplifier's bias is not disturbed) but passes the audio AC signal through to the amplifier. Every audio input jack you have ever plugged into has a coupling capacitor just behind it. Typical values: 1–10 µF for audio, 100 nF – 1 nF for RF.
ch1_5.blocksBypassCap: Bypass / decoupling (from a supply rail to ground). Put a capacitor from the chip's power pin to ground, very close to the chip. Steady DC supply sees an open circuit and continues to the chip unaffected; fast high-frequency noise on the rail sees a near-short to ground through the capacitor and is shunted away. A typical digital board has a 100 nF ceramic bypass cap at every IC plus one 10 µF electrolytic per section — the small cap handles fast transients, the big one buffers slower current swings. Skipping bypass caps is the single most common cause of «it works on the breadboard but fails on the scope» bugs.
ch1_5.blocksVisualAria: Two side-by-side waveform sketches. On the left, a slow square wave labelled input feeds a capacitor-and-resistor, and the output underneath is a near-DC line — the cap has integrated the slow edges out. On the right, a fast square wave labelled input feeds the same circuit, and the output underneath is almost identical to the input — the cap has passed the signal through with minimal change.
ch1_5.blocksVisualSlow: Slow input (low frequency, close to DC): the capacitor charges up fully and then blocks any further change. V_out settles near zero — this is the «blocks DC» half of the behaviour.
ch1_5.blocksVisualFast: Fast input (high frequency, well above the cutoff): the capacitor has no time to build up a blocking voltage, so the signal passes through almost untouched. V_out tracks V_in — this is the «passes AC» half.

builder.title: Capacitance builder — plates, spacing, dielectric
builder.description: Set the plate area, the spacing, and the dielectric material. The widget computes capacitance from the parallel-plate formula and autoscales the unit.
builder.hint: Try to get to 1 µF with an air-gap dielectric — you will need plates the size of a dinner table with the gap squeezed down to ≈ 0.02 mm (thinner than a human hair). Then switch to X7R and watch the same geometry pack 3000× more capacitance.

combine.title: Capacitors in series and parallel
combine.description: Two capacitors at once. Left column is the parallel total, right is the series equivalent — the mirror of the resistor rules.
combine.hint: Pick one value ten times larger than the other and the pattern inverts from the resistor case: the larger cap dominates in parallel, the smaller cap dominates in series.

rc.title: RC charge / discharge
rc.description: Set R and C, hit charge or discharge, watch the exponential. τ-gridlines mark each time constant.
rc.hint: The «5τ rule»: after five time constants, the capacitor is within 0.7 % of its final value and is treated as fully charged (or discharged) for engineering purposes.

ch1_5.keyTakeaway1: A capacitor stores charge on two plates separated by a dielectric: <nowrap><var>Q</var> = <var>C</var><var>V</var></nowrap>, or equivalently <nowrap><var>I</var> = <var>C</var> · d<var>V</var>/d<var>t</var></nowrap>. A farad is huge; almost every real cap is specified in µF, nF, or pF.
ch1_5.keyTakeaway2: Three capacitor families cover almost everything: ceramic (cheap, fast, X7R has DC-bias drift), film (precise and stable, physically bigger), electrolytic (huge values, polarised — reverse it and it fails).
ch1_5.keyTakeaway3: Parallel adds: <nowrap><var>C</var> = <var>C_{\mathrm{1}}</var> + <var>C_{\mathrm{2}}</var></nowrap>. Series combines reciprocally: <nowrap>1 / <var>C</var> = 1 / <var>C_{\mathrm{1}}</var> + 1 / <var>C_{\mathrm{2}}</var></nowrap>. In series, the smaller the capacitance, the larger the voltage across it — the opposite of the resistor rule.
ch1_5.keyTakeaway4: Stored energy: <nowrap><var>U</var> = ½ <var>C</var><var>V</var>²</nowrap>. The time constant of an RC network is τ = <nowrap><var>R</var> · <var>C</var></nowrap>; a capacitor reaches 63 % of its final voltage in 1τ and is «done» (> 99 %) in 5τ.
ch1_5.keyTakeaway5: Blocks DC, passes AC. Two canonical uses follow from that: <coupl>coupling capacitors</coupl> in series pass the signal AC while blocking DC offsets, and <bypass>bypass capacitors</bypass> from supply to ground shunt high-frequency noise to ground without disturbing the DC rail.
ch1_5.labGoal: Charge and discharge a capacitor through a resistor, measure the RC time constant with a stopwatch and a multimeter, and compare the measured τ to the computed <nowrap><var>R</var> · <var>C</var></nowrap>.
ch1_5.labEquip1: Two 1.5 V AA cells in a holder (3 V nominal) — or any 3–9 V source you have handy.
ch1_5.labEquip2: <multimeter>Multimeter</multimeter> with a DC-volts range, ideally with 2 V and 20 V scales so you can resolve a few millivolts at the end of the discharge.
ch1_5.labEquip3: A <breadboard>breadboard</breadboard> plus a stopwatch — any phone-app stopwatch will do.
ch1_5.labEquip4: A pencil and a notebook — you will record voltage at regular intervals and plot the curve.
ch1_5.labComp1: One 100 µF aluminium electrolytic (any voltage rating ≥ 16 V). Observe polarity — the longer lead is positive, the stripe marks the negative lead.
ch1_5.labComp2: One 10 kΩ resistor (any wattage) for the charge/discharge path. Optional: a 100 kΩ resistor to compare a slower timescale.
ch1_5.labComp3: A piece of wire or a toggle switch to use as a charge/discharge switch — a clean hand-touched connection is fine for this lab.
ch1_5.labStep1: Wire the charge path on the breadboard. Put the resistor in series between the battery's positive terminal and the capacitor's positive lead. Connect the capacitor's negative lead to the battery's negative terminal. Do not close the circuit yet — leave one end of the resistor dangling until step 3.
ch1_5.labStep2: Predict τ before measuring. With R = 10 kΩ and C = 100 µF, τ = 10·10³ · 100·10⁻⁶ = 1 s. So after 1 s the cap should reach 63 % of the supply voltage; after 5 s it should be fully charged. Measure the actual battery voltage across the holder first — worn AAs sit well below 1.5 V each — and use that as your V_{\mathrm{in}}.
ch1_5.labStep3: Put the multimeter probes across the capacitor (positive probe to the positive lead) and set it to a DC-volts range that covers V_{\mathrm{in}}. Close the charge circuit (touch the loose resistor lead to its terminal) and simultaneously start the stopwatch. Read the capacitor voltage at 1 s, 2 s, 3 s, 5 s, 10 s. Write each one down.
ch1_5.labStep4: Compare with theory. At 1 s you should read ≈ 63 % of <var>V_{\mathrm{in}}</var>; at 2 s, ≈ 86 %; at 3 s, ≈ 95 %; at 5 s, ≈ 99 %. Expect ±20 % disagreement — electrolytics routinely have that tolerance, plus your stopwatch trigger has a human-reaction-time bias of ~200 ms that matters for a 1-second timescale. If the cap charges in a blink (well under a second), you probably built an undersized RC — check <var>R</var> and <var>C</var>.
ch1_5.labStep5: Open the charge path and short the resistor across the capacitor terminals (or wire a second resistor between the cap terminals). You have just connected a discharge path. Start the stopwatch, read the cap voltage at 1 s, 2 s, 5 s. The curve should mirror step 3 — 37 %, 14 %, < 1 % of the starting voltage.
ch1_5.labStep6: Swap the 10 kΩ resistor for a 100 kΩ resistor and repeat. τ is now 10 s — you have plenty of time to take readings. The same 63 / 86 / 95 / 99 percentages apply, just stretched out ten times. Notice how all RC curves have the same shape; only the horizontal scale changes with τ.
ch1_5.labExpected: Measured charge-curve points agree with theory to within about ±20 %. The 100 kΩ run gives a curve ten times slower but identical in shape. You can now predict any RC charging or discharging scenario by computing τ = <nowrap><var>R</var> · <var>C</var></nowrap> and reading off the percentage table.
ch1_5.labConnection: The timescale you just measured is the same τ that sets the edge rise time of a digital signal through any RC parasitic, the cutoff frequency of an RC filter (<nowrap><var>f_{\mathrm{c}}</var> = 1 / (2π<var>R</var><var>C</var>)</nowrap>), the debounce delay on a switch, and the settling time of every analogue circuit you will meet later. Chapter 1.7 recasts this exact circuit in the frequency domain; Chapter 1.8 uses it to build low- and high-pass filters.
ch1_5.labTrouble1: The capacitor voltage rises instantly to V_{\mathrm{in}} the moment you close the circuit. You have a direct short — the resistor isn't actually in circuit, or the capacitor is much smaller than you think. Measure both in isolation with the multimeter's resistance / capacitance range.
ch1_5.labTrouble2: The voltage never quite reaches V_{\mathrm{in}} even after 10 s. Normal — after 5τ you are at 99.3 %, not 100 %. Also the electrolytic has leakage current that pulls down the final voltage by a fraction of a percent. Accept 99 %.
ch1_5.labTrouble3: The measured τ is well off the predicted value. Electrolytic capacitors have loose tolerance (±20 % to +100 %, often asymmetric — the foot-of-the-datasheet fine print) and their value drifts with age. Measure the actual C with a capacitance meter if your multimeter has one; you will likely find it is not exactly 100 µF.
ch1_5.quiz_q1: A 470 nF capacitor is in a circuit. Expressed in microfarads, how much capacitance is that?
ch1_5.quiz_q1_explanation: Nano is 10⁻⁹ and micro is 10⁻⁶, so 1 nF = 0.001 µF. 470 nF = 0.47 µF.
ch1_5.quiz_q2: Two 10 µF capacitors are connected in parallel. What is the equivalent capacitance?
ch1_5.quiz_q2_explanation: Capacitors in parallel add (the opposite of resistors): 10 µF + 10 µF = 20 µF.
ch1_5.quiz_q3: The same two 10 µF capacitors are now connected in series. What is the equivalent capacitance?
ch1_5.quiz_q3_explanation: Capacitors in series combine reciprocally (like resistors in parallel): 10·10 / (10 + 10) = 5 µF. Two equal caps in series always give half the value.
ch1_5.quiz_q4: A 10 kΩ resistor charges a 100 µF capacitor from a 5 V supply. What is the RC time constant?
ch1_5.quiz_q4_explanation: <nowrap>τ = <var>R</var> · <var>C</var> = 10·10³ · 100·10⁻⁶ = 1 s</nowrap>. Mnemonic: kΩ × µF = milliseconds, so 10 kΩ × 100 µF = 1000 ms = 1 s.
ch1_5.quiz_q5: In the circuit of question 4, what voltage does the capacitor reach after one time constant?
ch1_5.quiz_q5_explanation: After 1τ, any RC charge curve reaches 63.2 % of the supply. 5 V × 0.632 ≈ 3.16 V.
ch1_5.quiz_q6: You are installing a tantalum SMT capacitor. One end of the case has a stripe. Which end does the stripe mark?
ch1_5.quiz_q6_c: The end with the higher voltage rating.
ch1_5.quiz_q6_d: It doesn't matter — tantalums are bipolar.
ch1_5.quiz_q6_explanation: Tantalum convention is the opposite of aluminium: the stripe on a tantalum marks the positive lead. The oxide dielectric is grown in one direction, so reverse polarity can cause catastrophic failure. Verify with the datasheet if in any doubt.
ch1_5.quiz_q7: Which capacitor type is most appropriate for setting the frequency of an RF oscillator, where stability with temperature and voltage is critical?
ch1_5.quiz_q7_b: Aluminium electrolytic — large and readily available.
ch1_5.quiz_q7_c: C0G (NP0) ceramic — picofarad range, ±30 ppm/°C, negligible DC-bias drift.
ch1_5.quiz_q7_explanation: C0G / NP0 ceramics combine the small values typical of RF oscillator tanks with near-perfect temperature and voltage stability. X7R drops significantly with DC bias and temperature; electrolytics are both polarised and have high ESR — both are unacceptable in a precision oscillator.
ch1_5.quiz_q8: A 47 µF capacitor is charged to 400 V across a high-voltage power supply. A 470 kΩ bleeder resistor is wired across it. After the supply is switched off, roughly how long should you wait before considering the capacitor safe to touch (assuming the bleeder is intact)?
ch1_5.quiz_q8_explanation: τ = 470·10³ · 47·10⁻⁶ ≈ 22 s. 5τ ≈ 110 s — but this is the minimum, and only if the bleeder is actually working. Bleeders can fail open silently. Always verify with a voltmeter before touching the terminals of a large high-voltage capacitor.
ch1_5.seriesIslandAria: Illustration: two capacitors connected in series. The two inner plates — linked to each other by a single wire and to nothing else — are wrapped in a dashed cloud labelled «isolated node: total charge Σ = 0». The four plates carry charges +Q, −Q, +Q, −Q from left to right; battery terminals «+» and «−» sit at the two outer ends.
ch1_5.rcSchematicCaption: RC charging circuit. Battery <var>V_{\mathrm{in}}</var>, with switch S, resistor <var>R</var>, and capacitor <var>C</var> wired in series. The voltmeter alongside reads the voltage across the capacitor — this is <var>V_{\mathrm{C}}</var>, which we'll track over time.
ch1_5.rcSchematicAria: Schematic: battery <var>V_{\mathrm{in}}</var> on the left, switch S, resistor R and capacitor C in series along the top rail; bottom return rail brings current back to the battery’s negative terminal. A voltmeter sits in parallel with the capacitor, reading the voltage across it, labelled <var>V_{\mathrm{C}}</var>.
ch1_5.rcWorkedExampleProblem: Worked example — RC timing for a 555 timer. The 555 is a classic 8-pin IC used to generate single pulses and square waves. Internally it watches the voltage across an external RC network; when V_C crosses 2/3 of the supply voltage, the 555 flips its output from HIGH to LOW. So by picking R and C we can dial in the pulse length. Question: which R and C give a 1-second pulse?
ch1_5.rcWorkedExampleStep1: Step 1 — write down what «pulse ends» means. The 555 stops the pulse the instant the cap hits 2/3 of V_{\mathrm{in}}. The charge law of the RC circuit says V_C(t) = V_{\mathrm{in}}(1 − e^(−t/RC)). Set those two expressions equal at the pulse end time t:
ch1_5.rcWorkedExampleStep2: Step 2 — divide through by V_{\mathrm{in}}. The supply voltage cancels on both sides — the result doesn't depend on whether you power the 555 from 5 V or 9 V or 12 V. Neat.
ch1_5.rcWorkedExampleStep3: Step 3 — solve for t. Take the natural logarithm of both sides. On the left, ln and e−x cancel each other — they are inverse functions — and we are left with −t/RC. On the right, ln(1/3) is easier to read if we rewrite it: since 1/3 = 3−1 and the logarithm of a power obeys ln(xn) = n · ln(x), we get ln(1/3) = ln(3−1) = −ln 3. So both sides now carry a minus:
ch1_5.rcWorkedExampleStep4: Step 4 — plug in the target time. We want t = 1 s. The product RC must therefore be:
ch1_5.rcWorkedExampleStep5: Step 5 — pick real components. One equation, two unknowns: we have freedom. In practice, pick C first — capacitors come in far fewer off-the-shelf values than resistors, so starting with a common cap makes the resistor value easier to match from stock. Pick C = 10 µF (a common electrolytic) and derive R:
ch1_5.rcWorkedExampleStep6: 91 kΩ is an E24 series value — the standard set of ±5 % tolerance resistor values (10, 11, 12, 13, 15, 16, 18, 20, 22, 24, 27, 30, 33, 36, 39, 43, 47, 51, 56, 62, 68, 75, 82, 91 and their decade multiples). Any stock of components will have it. If you chose C = 1 µF instead, R would be 910 kΩ — also E24. The recipe «pick a round capacitor, derive the resistor» works because there's always an E24 value within a few percent of whatever R the maths demands.
ch1_5.rcWorkedExampleStep3b: Now the easy part. Multiply both sides by −1 — or equivalently flip the signs on both ends. The minuses drop away together:
ch1_5.blocksHighPassSchematicCaption: The high-pass RC filter used below. V_in enters on the left, the capacitor sits in series with the signal path, and V_out is tapped at the junction between C and R. The resistor R ties that node to ground so the capacitor has somewhere to discharge through.
ch1_5.couplingCapSchematicCaption: Interstage coupling. The microphone on the left has its signal referenced to 0 V. The amplifier on the right needs its input biased to +2 V for its operating point — that +2 V bias is set by the <var>R_{1}</var> / <var>R_{2}</var> divider from the supply V+. The capacitor C sits in series between them: it blocks the 2 V DC difference (nothing disturbs either stage’s operating point), while the AC audio signal passes through unimpeded.
ch1_5.bypassCapSchematicCaption: Bypass / decoupling. The chip is powered from V+ on top and GND on the bottom. The bypass capacitor C sits between the supply rail and GND, placed as close to the chip’s <var>V_{\mathrm{cc}}</var> pin as the PCB allows. DC supply current flows through the chip as usual (the cap is a break for DC), but any high-frequency noise on the rail sees the cap as an almost-short to GND and goes straight there instead of through the chip. Typical value: 100 nF ceramic per chip, plus one 10 µF electrolytic per board section.
