ch1_6.heroAriaLabel: A hand-drawn side view of an air-core inductor: a long horizontal cylinder of empty space (the core) with a row of bumps along its top edge representing the wire wraps. Inside the core, four horizontal arrows pointing right show the magnetic field B running along the solenoid's axis. Two leads exit at each end, with the left lead carrying a current arrow labelled I.
ch1_6.intro: An inductor is a device that stores energy in a magnetic field around a coil of wire. In Chapter 1.5 you met the capacitor: charge on plates, energy in an electric field, blocks DC and passes AC. The inductor is its mirror image — current through a coil, energy in a magnetic field, and the AC/DC roles swapped: it passes DC (a steady current threads the coil with no opposition once the field is established), opposes AC (changing current means a changing field, which fights back), and integrates voltage over time (the longer you hold a voltage across it, the more current builds up). Almost every dual you learn for capacitors has an inductor counterpart in this chapter — and a few twists where the parallel breaks down.
ch1_6.introPreview: We will start with the defining equation V = L · dI/dt and the henry, build physical intuition with a widget that lets you set the number of turns, the coil dimensions, and the core material, tour the four inductor types you will actually meet on the bench (air-core, ferrite, iron-powder, RF chokes), look at energy stored in the field and where the spark comes from when you open a switch carrying current, meet the RL time constant τ = L / R (the same exponential as RC, but with the roles of voltage and current swapped), combine inductors in series and parallel (this time the formulas are the same as resistors, not opposite), and close with «opposes AC, passes DC» — the inductor's defining trick — that sets up the reactance discussion in Chapter 1.7.
ch1_6.whatItIsIntro: Wind a wire into a coil and pass current through it. By the right-hand rule, that current produces a magnetic field threading through the centre of the coil and looping back around the outside. The field stores energy. Now change the current — speed it up, slow it down, reverse it — and the field, being proportional to the current, changes with it. By Faraday's law, a changing magnetic flux induces a voltage in any wire it threads — including the very wire that carries the changing current. This isn't a second current; it's an EMF the coil generates between its terminals, and Lenz's law gives its polarity: the induced voltage always opposes the change that caused it. Push the current up faster and the coil produces a back-voltage that fights the source driving it; let the current die away and the coil produces a voltage in the opposite direction that tries to keep it flowing. The closest analogy is mechanical inertia: a heavy cart resists changes in velocity — push to speed it up, brake to slow it down, and both moves meet pushback. Replace mass with the coil's electrical inertia, velocity with current, and force with voltage, and you have the same law in electrical form. That self-induced opposition to changing current is what we call inductance, symbol L.
ch1_6.whatItIsVdIdt: The defining equation says: the voltage across the coil is proportional to how fast the current through it is changing, and the constant of proportionality is the inductance:
ch1_6.whatItIsDefinitions: Inductance L is measured in henries (H). One henry is the inductance that produces one volt of induced opposition when the current through it changes at one ampere per second. A henry is a large unit — most inductors you will meet are specified in millihenries (mH = 10⁻³ H), microhenries (µH = 10⁻⁶ H), or nanohenries (nH = 10⁻⁹ H). A 1 H choke is a fist-sized iron-cored chunk for power-supply work; a 100 nH coil is a few turns of fine wire on a pencil for VHF.
ch1_6.whatItIsCompare: Compare the inductor's defining equation to the capacitor's: in a capacitor, current is proportional to the rate of change of voltage (<nowrap><var>I</var> = <var>C</var> · d<var>V</var>/d<var>t</var></nowrap>); in an inductor, voltage is proportional to the rate of change of current. The roles of <var>V</var> and <var>I</var> are exactly swapped — and that swap is the source of every duality between the two components in the rest of this chapter.
ch1_6.whatItIsRateNote: Voltage equals inductance times the rate of change of current. Read it both ways. If the current through an inductor is held steady, dI/dt = 0 and the voltage across it is zero — that is «passes DC», one equation deep. If the current changes, a voltage appears; faster changes demand more voltage — that is «opposes AC». Worked number: push 1 A of current through a 1 mH inductor and try to interrupt it in 1 µs (a typical switch-open transient). The induced voltage spikes to V = 1·10⁻³ · 1/(1·10⁻⁶) = 1000 V — far above the supply voltage, easily enough to arc across the contacts. This is why opening a switch carrying inductor current produces a spark, and why circuits with inductors need flyback diodes.
ch1_6.sectionGeometry: What sets the value — turns, geometry, core
ch1_6.geometryIntro: The inductance of an idealised <sol>solenoid</sol> (a long, evenly-wound coil) depends on four things — three from the geometry and one from the core material:
ch1_6.geometryVars: n is the number of turns; the inductance is proportional to n² — double the turns and you quadruple the inductance, because each turn both produces more field and gets more flux from the others. A is the cross-sectional area of the coil (more area ↔ more flux per turn). l is the length of the coil — and inductance is inversely proportional to l: stretching the same n turns over twice the length spaces them out twice as far apart, the field inside the coil drops by half, the flux per turn drops by half, and so does the inductance. Pack those same turns into half the length and you double the inductance instead. µ_{\mathrm{r}} is the relative permeability of the core material — a pure number that tells you how much better a material conducts magnetic flux than a vacuum. µ_{\mathrm{0}} is the permeability of free space, a physical constant ≈ 1.257 × 10⁻⁶ H/m.
ch1_6.geometryCores: A vacuum is the reference — µ_{\mathrm{r}} = 1. Air is essentially the same. Common core materials do dramatically better: iron-powder cores ≈ 10–100 (mostly used for RF and switching supplies); ferrites ≈ 100–10000 (broad RF range, EMI suppression); laminated iron / silicon steel ≈ 1000–10000 (mains-frequency transformers and chokes); permalloy and exotic alloys ≈ 10⁵ (specialised, expensive). The factor of 10000 between air and ferrite is exactly why a 1 H choke is fist-sized rather than house-sized.
ch1_6.geometryBuilderIntro: The widget below lets you set <var>n</var>, <var>A</var>, <var>l</var>, and pick a core material. It computes inductance from the solenoid formula and autoscales the unit (nH / µH / mH). Try to build 1 mH on an air-core former — even with a fist-sized coil and 100+ turns you will struggle. Then switch to a ferrite core and watch the same geometry pack a thousand times more inductance into the same volume.
ch1_6.sectionTypes: Types you will meet on the shelf
ch1_6.typesIntro: Inductors come in fewer flavours than capacitors but each family is tuned to a specific job — the differences come down to the core material. Four families cover almost everything you will wire up day-to-day in radio work. Specialty parts that are also coils — variometers, transformers, <balun>baluns</balun> and <unun>ununs</unun> — get their own dedicated treatment later: transformers in Chapter 1.9, and baluns/ununs in the antenna chapter (3.3), where every ham builds and uses them.
ch1_6.typesAirCoreIntro: Air-core inductors are simply turns of wire on an insulating former (or no former at all — wire stiff enough to hold its shape). Inductance is modest (nH to a few µH) but the core cannot saturate, the value is rock-stable across temperature and current, and losses are minimal at high frequency. These are the inductors you find inside RF tuned circuits in transmitters, antenna matching networks, and HF / VHF filters where geometric stability (and thus the exact value of L) matters more than cramming millihenries into a small package.
ch1_6.typesAirCoreCallout: Wind your own. Air-core RF coils are the one inductor type radio amateurs routinely make on the bench. Online calculators will turn (target inductance, wire gauge, former diameter) into «wind n turns, this many millimetres long». Common reference design: 10 turns of 1 mm enamelled copper wire on a 10 mm former, 15 mm long ≈ 0.7 µH — a perfectly reasonable VHF tank coil.
ch1_6.typesFerriteIntro: Ferrite-core inductors use a sintered ceramic of iron oxide mixed with manganese, zinc, or nickel — non-conductive, so eddy-current losses are tiny even at MHz frequencies. The high µ_{\mathrm{r}} (1000–10000) means high inductance per turn, so a few turns on a small toroid can give millihenries. Ferrites are the standard choice for switching power supplies, EMI suppression chokes (the cylindrical lump on USB cables and PC monitor leads is a ferrite bead), and small-signal RF transformers and baluns. The catch: ferrites saturate above a critical flux density, after which their effective permeability drops abruptly and the inductor stops behaving as one — important for any DC-carrying application.
ch1_6.typesIronPowderIntro: Iron-powder cores mix microscopic iron particles into an insulating binder, giving low-to-moderate permeability (10–100) but a very gradual, soft saturation curve — they tolerate large DC currents without abruptly losing inductance. The sweet spot is RF amplifier output networks, antenna tuners, and switching-supply chokes that have to carry significant DC bias along with the AC component. Toroidal iron-powder cores are colour-coded by mix (T-50-2 «red» for HF, T-50-6 «yellow» for VHF, etc.) — the colour identifies the frequency range the mix is optimised for.
ch1_6.typesChokesIntro: RF chokes are inductors specifically designed to block RF while passing DC. A typical RF choke is a few hundred microhenries to several millihenries — enough that at the frequencies of interest its impedance is much higher than the surrounding circuit, so RF currents are blocked; but at DC, it is just a wire. Chokes appear in the supply lines feeding RF amplifiers (so the supply doesn't short the RF out to ground), in feedlines to bias active antennas, and as the inductor in lots of LC filters. Most are factory-wound on a bobbin and look like a short, thick resistor with a value-and-tolerance code printed on the side.
ch1_6.typesGalleryAria: A hand-drawn row of six labelled inductor silhouettes side by side: a cylindrical coil of wire wound on no former («air-core»), a cylindrical coil wound on a black rod («ferrite rod»), a doughnut-shaped wire-wrapped torus («toroid»), a small cylinder with stripes like a resistor («moulded RF choke»), a fist-sized iron-laminated transformer-like core («laminated iron»), and a tiny rectangular SMD chip with two end-cap contacts («SMD inductor»). Each silhouette is labelled underneath.
ch1_6.energyIntro: When you push current into an inductor against its self-induced opposition, you do work. That work is not dissipated as heat (an ideal inductor doesn't dissipate anything); it is stored in the magnetic field around the coil. The amount, derived by integrating V · I · dt from zero current to the final current, is:
ch1_6.energyWorkedExample: A small 1 mH choke carrying 100 mA stores <nowrap><var>U</var> = 0.5 · 1·10⁻³ · (0.1)² = 5 µJ</nowrap> — tiny. But a 10 mH choke in a switching supply carrying 10 A stores <nowrap>0.5 · 10·10⁻³ · 10² = 0.5 J</nowrap> — modest, but try interrupting that current in a microsecond and the resulting voltage spike will be measured in kilovolts.
ch1_6.energySparkCallout: Where the spark comes from. The defining equation V = L · dI/dt is also a warning. An ideal switch opens in zero time — dI/dt goes to infinity, and so does the induced voltage (back-EMF). In practice, the voltage rises until something breaks down: the air gap between the contacts ionises and a spark carries the current across, dissipating the stored energy as heat and light. This is why mechanical relays carrying inductive loads have a flyback diode wired across the coil, why ignition coils in petrol engines work by deliberately interrupting current through a heavily-inductive primary winding, and why you should never disconnect a battery while a high-power transmitter is on key — the supply leads themselves are inductive enough to spark.
ch1_6.energySparkCalloutMechanism: How the flyback diode does it. Wire it cathode to the +V rail, anode to the switched end of the coil. During normal operation the diode is reverse-biased and invisible to the circuit. The instant the switch opens, the coil's back-EMF flips the polarity across the diode and forward-biases it; current that was flowing through the coil now circulates through the diode loop and decays smoothly while the coil's own resistance dissipates the stored energy as heat. The contact voltage never exceeds the supply voltage plus one diode drop (≈ 0.7 V) — instead of the kilovolts a bare inductor would generate.
ch1_6.rlIntro: Wire a resistor R in series with an inductor L across a supply V_{\mathrm{in}}. Close the switch. In the very first instant the current is zero and the inductor's defining equation says all of V_{\mathrm{in}} appears as a voltage across L — none across R. As current ramps up, the voltage on R grows (V_R = IR), the voltage on L shrinks to V_{\mathrm{in}} − V_R, the rate of change tapers, and the current creeps ever more slowly toward its steady-state value I_{\mathrm{∞}} = V_{\mathrm{in}} / R but never quite reaches it. The waveform is the same exponential as RC — only this time it is the current that climbs, not the voltage:
ch1_6.rlTauDefn: The ratio L / R has units of seconds (H / Ω = s — chase the SI units through to verify) and is, like its RC cousin, called the time constant, symbol τ. It sets the whole timescale of the current-rise curve. Two handy shortcuts to memorise:
ch1_6.rlShortcuts: mH / Ω = milliseconds. µH / kΩ = nanoseconds. A 1 mH choke fed through a 100 Ω resistor has τ = 10 µs. A 10 µH coil through a 10 Ω resistor has τ = 1 µs.
ch1_6.rlCurvePoints: At t = τ, the current has reached 63.2 % of its steady-state value. At 2τ, 86.5 %. At 3τ, 95.0 %. At 4τ, 98.2 %. At 5τ, 99.3 % — the same «five time constants» rule from RC. After 5τ the inductor is essentially «full» (or, on the discharge path, essentially empty). Same shape, same percentages, same rule of thumb.
ch1_6.rlDischargeDerivation: Discharge is symmetric — but with a hazard. Open the switch on a charged inductor and the current would suddenly fall to zero except for one thing: the inductor, by its defining equation, refuses to let the current change instantaneously. It generates whatever voltage is needed to keep the current flowing, even briefly. If a discharge path exists (a resistor in parallel with the coil, or a flyback diode), the current decays along the same time constant τ = L / R:
ch1_6.rlWidgetIntro: The widget below lets you pick L and R, hit «charge» or «discharge», and watch the exponential current trace with gridlines at each τ. Notice that doubling L doubles τ but doubling R halves it (the opposite of RC) — because here R sits in the denominator of the time constant.
ch1_6.rlVoltageNote: Note on what's plotted. In RC we tracked V_C rising. In RL the natural variable is the current — which is what the widget plots. If you instead measured the voltage across the inductor it would mirror the discharge curve (V_L starts at V_{\mathrm{in}} and decays to zero as the current rises). The choice of axis is just convention: «what does the energy-storage variable look like?» — for a capacitor that's voltage on the plates, for an inductor it's current through the coil.
ch1_6.combinationsIntro: Wire two inductors together and the same two arrangements apply — series (end-to-end, same current through both) or parallel (shared end nodes, same voltage across both). The formulas are the same as resistors: inductors add in series and combine reciprocally in parallel. This is the opposite of capacitors — and the duality with caps is exact, because the V↔I roles between the two components are swapped.
ch1_6.seriesFormulaLead: Series. Two inductors in series share the same current. The voltage across each is V_i = L_i · dI/dt; the total voltage is the sum. So the equivalent inductance is just the sum of the two:
ch1_6.seriesIntuition: The series total is always larger than either part — you are stacking turns. Two 1 mH chokes in series give 2 mH, just like resistors.
ch1_6.parallelFormulaLead: Parallel. Two inductors in parallel share the same voltage. The current through each is found by integrating V/L_i; the total current is the sum. The equivalent inductance satisfies the reciprocal sum:
ch1_6.parallelShortcut: For two inductors only, the reciprocal form collapses to a product-over-sum:
ch1_6.parallelIntuition: The parallel total is always smaller than either part. Two 1 mH chokes in parallel give 0.5 mH — exactly mirroring how two equal resistors in parallel give half.
ch1_6.combinationsCouplingNote: One important caveat — coupling. The formulas above assume the two inductors are not magnetically coupled to each other (no shared flux). Mount two inductors side by side with their cores aligned and their fields will couple — and the effective inductance no longer matches the formula. The full treatment with mutual inductance M waits for Chapter 1.9 (Transformers); for now, keep the two inductors physically separated or oriented at right angles when you want them to behave independently.
ch1_6.combinationsSchematicAria: Two side-by-side inductor schematics. On the left, two inductors L-one and L-two drawn in series along a single wire, with a formula L equals L-one plus L-two underneath. On the right, the same two inductors drawn in parallel between two terminals with a formula L equals L-one times L-two divided by L-one plus L-two underneath.
ch1_6.combinationsCalcIntro: The calculator below runs both formulas on whatever pair of values you enter. Notice that mismatched values follow the same rule as resistors: the larger inductor dominates in series, the smaller dominates in parallel — exactly the opposite of the capacitor pattern.
ch1_6.sectionBlocksAcPassesDc: «Opposes AC, passes DC» — a first look
ch1_6.blocksIntro: The defining equation gives the inductor's other key behaviour for free. V = L · dI/dt: the voltage across an inductor is proportional to the rate of change of the current. Pass a steady DC current through it and, once everything has settled, the current stops changing — dI/dt = 0 — so no voltage drop. The inductor is acting as a plain wire to DC.
ch1_6.blocksOpposesAc: Pass an AC current through the same inductor and dI/dt is nonzero throughout every cycle — a voltage builds up across it. The higher the frequency, the faster the current changes, the larger the opposition — so the inductor looks more and more like a high impedance as frequency rises. The quantitative story is inductive reactance, X_{\mathrm{L}} = 2πfL: an impedance in ohms that rises linearly with both inductance and frequency. At 1 kHz a 1 mH inductor looks like ≈ 6.3 Ω; at 1 MHz the same inductor looks like 6.3 kΩ — a thousand times more opposition. Compare to a capacitor whose reactance falls with frequency: the two components are perfect duals. Chapter 1.7 uses both together to build resonance, filters, and tuned circuits.
ch1_6.blocksApplications: Two applications of «opposes AC, passes DC» dominate practical circuits.
ch1_6.blocksRfChoke: RF choke (in series with the supply path). An RF amplifier is fed DC from the supply rail through an RF choke. The choke passes the DC supply current through to the transistor (no resistance, no voltage drop), but its high impedance at the operating RF frequency keeps the RF energy from leaking back into the supply rail and getting shorted to ground via the supply's bypass capacitors. Every RF amplifier has at least one choke per stage, sized so its reactance at the operating frequency is much larger than the load impedance the stage drives.
ch1_6.blocksFilterChoke: Filter choke (in series with a noisy DC line). A power-supply filter inductor is wired in series between a rectifier and the load. It opposes the AC ripple component (which has nonzero dI/dt) while passing the DC current with no resistive drop. Combined with a filter capacitor across the load, you get the classic LC low-pass filter — the standard topology for cleaning up rectified mains-frequency ripple in linear supplies, or switching-frequency ripple in switch-mode supplies. The same arrangement, scaled smaller, is also how speaker crossovers split bass from treble.
ch1_6.blocksVisualAria: Two side-by-side waveform sketches. On the left, a slow square-wave current is drawn through an inductor and a small voltage spike appears at each edge of the input — the inductor opposes only the change. On the right, a fast AC current through the same inductor produces a much larger voltage that tracks the rate of change throughout — the higher frequency, the higher the voltage.
ch1_6.blocksVisualSlow: Slow current change (low frequency, close to DC): the inductor only develops a brief voltage at each transition; in between it acts like a wire — this is the «passes DC» half of the behaviour.
ch1_6.blocksVisualFast: Fast current change (high frequency, well above the cutoff): the inductor develops a continuous voltage proportional to the current's rate of change, blocking AC propagation through it — this is the «opposes AC» half.
ch1_6.keyTakeaway1: An inductor stores energy in a magnetic field around a coil: <nowrap><var>V</var> = <var>L</var> · d<var>I</var>/d<var>t</var></nowrap>. A henry is huge; almost every real inductor is specified in mH, µH, or nH.
ch1_6.keyTakeaway2: Four inductor families cover almost everything: air-core (RF, dimensionally stable, no saturation), ferrite (high permeability, EMI suppression, can saturate under DC bias), iron-powder (gradual saturation, RF amps), and RF chokes (factory-wound to block RF while passing DC).
ch1_6.keyTakeaway3: Series adds: <nowrap><var>L</var> = <var>L_{\mathrm{1}}</var> + <var>L_{\mathrm{2}}</var></nowrap>. Parallel combines reciprocally: <nowrap>1 / <var>L</var> = 1 / <var>L_{\mathrm{1}}</var> + 1 / <var>L_{\mathrm{2}}</var></nowrap>. Same as resistors, opposite of capacitors. Holds only when the two inductors are not magnetically coupled.
ch1_6.keyTakeaway4: Stored energy: <nowrap><var>U</var> = ½ <var>L</var><var>I</var>²</nowrap>. The time constant of an RL network is τ = <nowrap><var>L</var> / <var>R</var></nowrap>; the current reaches 63 % of its final value in 1τ and is «done» (> 99 %) in 5τ.
ch1_6.keyTakeaway5: Opposes AC, passes DC. Reactance <nowrap><var>X_{\mathrm{L}}</var> = 2π<var>f</var><var>L</var></nowrap> rises with frequency — the dual of capacitive reactance, which falls with frequency. RF chokes use this to block RF while passing DC supply current; LC filters use it to clean up rectified or switched DC.
ch1_6.labGoal: Build a simple RL circuit, observe the exponential current rise on an oscilloscope, measure the time constant, and compute the inductance from <nowrap>τ = <var>L</var> / <var>R</var></nowrap>.
ch1_6.labEquip1: <arduino>Arduino</arduino> Uno (or compatible) — used as a square-wave source on a PWM pin, the same as the ch0.2 / ch1.5 labs.
ch1_6.labEquip2: <oscilloscope>Oscilloscope</oscilloscope> with one probe — to view the voltage across the resistor (which is proportional to current).
ch1_6.labEquip4: Around 1 m of 0.3 mm enamelled copper wire (any thin magnet wire, sometimes labelled «winding wire») — for the coil. A drinking straw or a marker pen as a winding former.
ch1_6.labComp1: One 100 Ω resistor for the current-limiting / sense path. Any wattage will do; this is a small-signal lab.
ch1_6.labComp2: One flyback diode (1N4148, 1N4001, or any general-purpose diode) — wired across the coil to clamp the open-circuit voltage spike when the Arduino's PWM swings low.
ch1_6.labComp3: Approximately 50 turns of magnet wire wound on the chosen former. Strip the enamel from the ends with sandpaper or a flame; the freshly bare copper has to make solder-tight contact in the breadboard.
ch1_6.labStep1: Wind the coil. Wrap the enamelled wire neatly around the former — count the turns. Keep the turns close together; gaps lower the inductance. Leave at least 5 cm of wire as leads at each end. Carefully strip 1 cm of enamel from both ends with sandpaper. Test with a multimeter: a properly stripped coil should read a few ohms (just the wire's DC resistance), not «OL».
ch1_6.labStep2: Predict the inductance. For a single-layer solenoid of n turns, length l cm, and diameter d cm, a useful empirical formula is L ≈ d² · n² / (l + 0.45 d) µH (Wheeler's formula) — a few-percent-accurate approximation as long as the coil is at least roughly as long as it is wide. For 50 turns on a 6 mm (0.6 cm) marker pen, 25 mm (2.5 cm) long: L ≈ 0.36 · 2500 / (2.5 + 0.27) = 900 / 2.77 ≈ 325 µH. A coil of this rough size will land in the hundreds-of-µH range; we will compare measurement to prediction at the end.
ch1_6.labStep3: Wire the circuit. Arduino pin 9 → 100 Ω resistor → coil → Arduino GND. Wire the flyback diode in parallel with the coil, with the cathode (banded end) toward the resistor side and the anode toward GND. Place the oscilloscope probe across the resistor (probe tip on the resistor's «coil» end, ground clip on the GND rail). The voltage you'll see is V_R(t) = I(t) · 100 Ω — i.e. proportional to current.
ch1_6.labStep4: Run a square-wave through pin 9. Upload the same Blink-style sketch from chapter 0.2, but at a frequency in the 100–1000 Hz range so each ON/OFF half-period is at least ten times longer than the predicted τ — that ensures the current reaches steady state on each half cycle. tone(9, 500) in setup() gives a 500 Hz square wave. Observe the trace: each rising edge of the input drives an exponential current rise; the trace climbs to a flat top, then drops sharply when pin 9 goes low (the diode now carries the discharge).
ch1_6.labStep5: Measure the time constant. Use the oscilloscope cursors. Find the steady-state voltage at the top of the rise (call it V_∞); set one cursor at the start of the rise and one where the trace reaches 63.2 % of V_∞. The time difference is τ. Read off the value — typically tens to hundreds of microseconds for a hundreds-of-µH coil and a 100 Ω resistor.
ch1_6.labStep6: Compute the inductance. Rearrange τ = L / R to get L = τ · R. With τ = 100 µs and R = 100 Ω, that's L = 10 mH. Compare to the value you predicted in step 2 — agreement within ±30 % is good for a hand-wound coil; the empirical formula assumes ideal geometry that real coils don't quite achieve.
ch1_6.labExpected: A clean exponential rise on the scope, a measurable τ, and a calculated <var>L</var> within the same order of magnitude as Wheeler's prediction. You'll have built and characterised your first inductor without ever owning an inductance meter.
ch1_6.labConnection: The same 63 % / 5τ relationship you measured for capacitor charging in ch1.5 also governs current rise in a coil — same exponential, same shortcut, same intuition. The reactance treatment in chapter 1.7 will recast this exact circuit in the frequency domain; chapter 1.8 uses RL filters as the dual of RC filters; chapter 1.9 takes two coupled inductors and gets a transformer.
ch1_6.labTrouble1: The current jumps instantly to its final value with no visible exponential. Either your coil's inductance is way smaller than expected (τ shorter than the scope can resolve at the chosen sweep rate), or you accidentally connected the resistor across the coil leads instead of in series. Slow the scope's time-base or wind more turns.
ch1_6.labTrouble2: You see a huge voltage spike at the falling edge of the input. The flyback diode is missing, reversed, or open. The spike can damage the Arduino — power off and re-check the diode orientation (cathode/banded-end toward the resistor side, away from GND).
ch1_6.labTrouble3: The trace looks scrappy or shifted. The coil's leads are not making good contact — bare-copper-into-breadboard joints are notoriously flaky. Re-strip the enamel with sandpaper, soldering an extra short tinned-copper lead onto each end if needed, and re-seat firmly.
ch1_6.quiz_q1: A 470 µH inductor is in a circuit. Expressed in millihenries, how much inductance is that?
ch1_6.quiz_q1_explanation: Micro is 10⁻⁶ and milli is 10⁻³, so 1 µH = 0.001 mH. 470 µH = 0.47 mH.
ch1_6.quiz_q2: Two 10 mH inductors are connected in series, far enough apart that they don't share any magnetic flux. What is the equivalent inductance?
ch1_6.quiz_q2_explanation: Inductors in series add (the same as resistors, the opposite of capacitors): 10 mH + 10 mH = 20 mH.
ch1_6.quiz_q3: The same two 10 mH inductors are now connected in parallel. What is the equivalent inductance?
ch1_6.quiz_q3_explanation: Inductors in parallel combine reciprocally (just like resistors in parallel): 10·10 / (10 + 10) = 5 mH. Two equal inductors in parallel always give half.
ch1_6.quiz_q4: A 10 mH inductor is fed from a 5 V supply through a 100 Ω resistor. What is the RL time constant?
ch1_6.quiz_q4_explanation: <nowrap>τ = <var>L</var> / <var>R</var> = 10·10⁻³ / 100 = 10⁻⁴ s = 100 µs</nowrap>. Mnemonic: mH ÷ Ω = ms (here 10 mH ÷ 100 Ω = 0.1 ms = 100 µs).
ch1_6.quiz_q5: In the circuit of question 4, what current does the inductor carry one time constant after the switch closes?
ch1_6.quiz_q5_explanation: Steady-state current is <nowrap><var>V</var>/<var>R</var> = 5 / 100 = 50 mA</nowrap>. After 1τ, any RL current curve reaches 63.2 %, so 50 × 0.632 ≈ 32 mA.
ch1_6.quiz_q6: A relay coil is being switched ON and OFF by a transistor. What single component, wired across the coil, prevents the open-circuit voltage spike from damaging the transistor?
ch1_6.quiz_q6_b: A flyback diode (cathode toward the supply, anode toward the transistor).
ch1_6.quiz_q6_explanation: When the transistor opens, the inductor tries to keep current flowing, generating a large reverse-polarity voltage. A flyback diode (also called a freewheel diode), reverse-biased to the supply during normal operation, becomes forward-biased when the spike polarity reverses, and clamps the spike to one diode-drop above the supply. Without it, a small relay coil can produce hundreds of volts at switch-off — easily enough to destroy a transistor rated for the supply only.
ch1_6.quiz_q7: An RF amplifier feeds its output transistor's collector through an RF choke to a 12 V supply rail. At the amplifier's operating frequency of 14 MHz, what role does the choke play?
ch1_6.quiz_q7_a: It boosts the supply voltage to compensate for losses.
ch1_6.quiz_q7_b: It looks like a near-short to ground for the RF, removing it from the output.
ch1_6.quiz_q7_c: It looks like a high impedance to the RF (preventing it from being shorted via the supply rail) while passing the DC supply current undisturbed.
ch1_6.quiz_q7_d: It cancels out the inductance of the supply leads.
ch1_6.quiz_q7_explanation: An RF choke is sized so <nowrap><var>X_L</var> = 2π<var>f</var><var>L</var></nowrap> at the operating frequency is much larger than the surrounding load impedance — typically tens of kΩ for HF stages. That high reactance prevents the RF from leaking back through the supply (and being shorted to ground via the supply's bypass caps), while at DC it's just a wire passing collector current.
ch1_6.quiz_q8: Two 1 mH inductors are mounted close together on a PCB with their cores aligned. The catalog formula predicts 0.5 mH for the parallel combination. The actual measured value is much different. Why?
ch1_6.quiz_q8_b: Inductors in parallel actually add, not combine reciprocally.
ch1_6.quiz_q8_c: The two inductors are magnetically coupled; the simple parallel formula assumes zero mutual inductance.
ch1_6.quiz_q8_d: One of the inductors is open.
ch1_6.quiz_q8_explanation: When two inductors are physically close and oriented the same way, their magnetic fields couple. The full equation includes a mutual-inductance term <var>M</var> that the catalog formula omits. Either separate the parts physically, mount them at right angles to each other, or compute the proper coupled-inductor result (which we'll cover in chapter 1.9 on transformers).

builder.title: Inductance builder — turns, geometry, core
builder.description: Set the number of turns, the coil dimensions, and the core material. The widget computes the inductance from the solenoid formula and autoscales the unit.
builder.hint: Try to get to 1 mH on an air-core former — even a fist-sized coil with 100 turns will struggle. Then switch to high-permeability ferrite and watch the same geometry pack thousands of times more inductance.

combine.title: Inductors in series and parallel
combine.description: Two inductors at once. Left column is the series total, right is the parallel equivalent — same arithmetic as resistors, opposite of capacitors.
combine.hint: Pick one value ten times larger than the other and the pattern matches the resistor case (and inverts the capacitor pattern): the larger inductor dominates in series, the smaller dominates in parallel.

rl.title: RL charge / discharge
rl.description: Set L and R, hit charge or discharge, watch the current trace. τ-gridlines mark each time constant.
rl.hint: Doubling L doubles τ, just like doubling C doubled τ in RC. But doubling R now halves τ — because here R sits in the denominator, the opposite of RC.

ch1_6.rlSchematicCaption: RL charging circuit. Battery <var>V_{\mathrm{in}}</var>, switch S, resistor <var>R</var>, and inductor <var>L</var> wired in series. The ammeter inline reads the current through the inductor — this is <var>I</var>(t), which we'll track over time.
ch1_6.rlSchematicAria: Schematic: battery <var>V_{\mathrm{in}}</var> on the left, switch S, resistor R, and inductor L in series along the top rail; bottom return rail brings current back to the battery's negative terminal. An ammeter sits inline with the loop, reading the loop current, labelled <var>I</var>.
ch1_6.rfChokeSchematicCaption: RF choke in an amplifier supply line. The transistor's collector is fed from V+ through a high-inductance RF choke. At DC the choke looks like a wire — supply current passes unrestricted. At the RF operating frequency the choke is many kΩ — RF generated at the collector can't escape back through the supply rail. A bypass capacitor on the supply rail completes the picture by shunting any residual RF to ground.
ch1_6.filterChokeSchematicCaption: LC low-pass filter — a textbook power-supply ripple filter. The inductor in series opposes any AC component (including ripple) while passing the DC current; the capacitor in parallel with the load shorts any remaining AC to ground. Together they cut ripple by orders of magnitude per LC stage.
ch1_6.rightHandRuleAria: Two side-by-side panels illustrating the right-hand rule. Left: a vertical straight wire carries current upward (arrow labelled I); four nested elliptical magnetic-field lines (B) circle the wire, with arrowheads showing they curl counter-clockwise as viewed from above. Right: a horizontal solenoid (cylindrical core with eight wire turns) carries current entering from the left lead and exiting on the right; a thick horizontal arrow labelled B inside the solenoid points along the axis to the right, and a faint dashed return-loop closes the field outside the coil. Each panel has a caption explaining the rule.
ch1_6.rightHandRuleLeftCaption: Straight wire — field B circles the wire
ch1_6.rightHandRuleRightCaption: Coil — field B runs along the axis
ch1_6.faradayDemoAria: Two side-by-side panels showing Faraday's law in action. Left: a bar magnet (S–N) sits stationary near the left end of a coil; the coil's leads connect to a galvanometer (a circle marked G with a needle inside); the needle points straight up — zero current. Right: the same setup, but the bar magnet is moving toward the coil (a horizontal arrow labelled v above the magnet, motion lines behind it). The galvanometer needle is now deflected to the side, and a curved arrow on the coil shows the induced current direction. Captions explain that the change in magnetic flux induces a voltage that drives a current.
ch1_6.solenoidGeometryAria: A 3D-perspective diagram of a solenoid showing the four geometric quantities in the inductance formula: A is the cross-sectional area, marked on the round face at the left end of the cylinder; l is the length of the coil, shown as a dimension arrow below; n is the number of turns, with a leader line pointing to one wrap; and µᵣ is the relative permeability of the core, labelled inside the cylinder.

ch1_7.heroAriaLabel: Pen-and-ink sketch of a parallel LC tank circuit. A four-sided rectangular wire loop. The top branch is split around a pair of vertical parallel-plate capacitor symbols, with three short dashed E-field lines between the plates and tiny + and − polarity marks above. The bottom branch is split around a side-view solenoid: a small horizontal cylinder with a wire visibly winding around it as a row of half-circle bumps. C and L labels sit above and below their respective components. The formula f₀ = 1 / (2π√LC) is written along the bottom edge.
ch1_7.intro: A capacitor stores energy in an electric field; an inductor stores it in a magnetic field. Wire the two together and the energy doesn't sit still — it sloshes back and forth between the capacitor's plates and the coil's flux at one specific frequency. That frequency is determined entirely by the values of L and C, has a name — the resonant frequency — and a formula: f_0 = 1 / (2π√(LC)). Resonance is the single most important phenomenon in radio: every receiver picks one station out of thousands by being resonant at that station's frequency, every transmitter relies on a resonant tank to clean up its output, and every antenna is, ultimately, a resonant LC structure with the L and C distributed along its length. The next two parts of this course are largely a study of what you can do with two passives once you put them in a loop.
ch1_7.introPreview: We will build the picture from the ground up. First, the swing analogy and the physical mechanism — why energy oscillates between L and C unprompted, and what sets the natural frequency. Then the resonance condition X_L = X_C — the algebraic counterpart of the same idea — with a calculator that takes L and C and returns f_0 (and vice versa). Then the two LC topologies side by side: series LC presents a near-short to its resonant frequency (the trap), parallel LC presents a near-open (the tank). The Q factor and bandwidth tell you how sharp the response is — both follow from the same reactance-to-loss ratio. We finish on the bench: feed the LC you wound in chapter 1.6 into a VNA, sweep the frequency, and watch the S11 dip mark f_0 with markers you can read off the screen — the same workflow every ham uses when checking an antenna or a homebrew filter.
ch1_7.swingIntro: Pull a child's swing back, let go, and it oscillates at one specific frequency — set by the rope length and gravity, not by how hard you pulled. The energy sloshes between two reservoirs each cycle: at the bottom of the arc all of it is kinetic (the swing is moving fast); at the top of each side all of it is potential (the swing is momentarily still, but lifted). With no friction, the swing would oscillate forever; in reality, air drag and bearing friction slowly drain the energy and the swing eventually stops. To keep it going you push at the right moment in each cycle — tuned to the natural frequency. Push out of step and the swing barely moves.
ch1_7.swingTransition: An LC circuit is the electrical version of exactly this picture. The two reservoirs are the capacitor and the inductor. The «motion» is the current circulating around the loop. There is one natural frequency at which energy passes freely between the two stores. Push the loop with an external source at that frequency and the response is dramatic; push at any other frequency and almost nothing happens. The push-at-the-right-moment idea is what every radio receiver does — it amplifies the one frequency the LC tank will respond to, and ignores the rest.
ch1_7.sectionSlosh: Energy sloshing in an LC loop
ch1_7.sloshIntro: Charge a capacitor to some voltage <var>V_0</var>, disconnect the source, and connect the capacitor across an inductor. What happens?
ch1_7.sloshStep1: t = 0. All the energy is in the capacitor's electric field — U_C = ½CV_0². The inductor carries no current. The capacitor «wants» to discharge through the inductor.
ch1_7.sloshStep2: The capacitor drives current through the inductor. But the inductor opposes any change in current — V_L = L · dI/dt — so the current builds up gradually. As it builds, the capacitor voltage falls. At the moment the capacitor voltage hits zero, the current is at its maximum, and ALL the energy has moved into the inductor's magnetic field — U_L = ½LI_{\mathrm{max}}².
ch1_7.sloshStep3: The inductor refuses to let the current stop instantly. Even with the capacitor empty, the inductor's flux drives the same current onward — and now charges the capacitor in the opposite polarity. The current decays as the new capacitor voltage builds against it. When the current reaches zero, all the energy is back in the capacitor — but with the polarity reversed.
ch1_7.sloshStep4: The cycle reverses. The capacitor (now charged the other way) drives current the other way through the inductor. After one more half-cycle we are back where we started: capacitor charged to V_0, current zero. The whole loop has taken one period T.
ch1_7.sloshFreq: The frequency of the cycle is set by L and C alone. A small L and small C means little inertia and little stored charge, so the cycle completes quickly — high frequency. A large L and large C means more electrical inertia and more charge to move, so the cycle takes longer — low frequency. The exact relationship comes from the equations above and the resonance condition we work out in the next section. In an ideal lossless LC, the cycle would repeat forever; in a real one, the inductor's winding resistance dissipates a little energy each cycle, and the oscillation gradually decays — exactly like the swing's air drag.
ch1_7.conditionIntro: From chapters 1.5 and 1.6 we have the two reactances: capacitive <nowrap><var>X_C</var> = 1 / (2π<var>f</var><var>C</var>)</nowrap> falls as frequency rises, and inductive <nowrap><var>X_L</var> = 2π<var>f</var><var>L</var></nowrap> rises with frequency. Plot them on the same axes and the two curves cross at exactly one frequency — the frequency where they are equal in magnitude:
ch1_7.conditionDerive: Setting <nowrap><var>X_L</var> = <var>X_C</var></nowrap> and solving for <var>f</var> is a one-line algebra exercise:
ch1_7.conditionFormulaIntro: Rearrange and you get the resonance formula stated at the top of the chapter:
ch1_7.conditionMeaning: This is the same frequency the energy-sloshing argument predicts. The two derivations are two views of the same physics: in the time domain we look at energy passing back and forth; in the frequency domain we look at the two reactances cancelling. Both produce the same f_0.
ch1_7.conditionScalingNote: Read the formula for its scaling, not just its number. f_0 depends on the square root of the LC product. To halve the resonant frequency you have to quadruple either component (or any combination whose product quadruples). Doubling L alone drops f_0 by a factor of √2 ≈ 1.41 — not 2. This is also why real adjustable tuned circuits use a variable capacitor (or a variable inductor) of about 10:1 range to cover a 3:1 frequency range.
ch1_7.conditionCalcIntro: The calculator below runs the formula in either direction. Enter L and C to get f_0; enter f_0 and one of L or C to get the other. It also reports the characteristic impedance Z_0 = √(L/C) — the magnitude of the reactance of either component at resonance, which sets the «impedance scale» of the tank and matters when you pair it with a 50 Ω source or load.
ch1_7.seriesIntro: Wire <var>L</var> and <var>C</var> end-to-end in a single loop and feed the loop from a source. At any single frequency the impedance the source sees is <nowrap><var>X_L</var> − <var>X_C</var></nowrap> (with the right signs — they oppose each other because L lags voltage and C leads it). At resonance the two cancel — leaving only the small loss resistance of the wire and core. Off-resonance, whichever reactance is larger dominates: below <var>f_0</var> the capacitor's <var>X_C</var> dominates, the loop looks capacitive; above <var>f_0</var> the inductor's <var>X_L</var> dominates, the loop looks inductive.
ch1_7.seriesResonanceBehaviour: At f_0 a series LC looks like a near-short circuit. Current is at its maximum (limited only by losses), and the source sees almost no impedance from the LC pair. Move slightly off f_0 and the impedance rises sharply — the rate of rise set by Q. A high-Q series LC is a sharp notch; a low-Q series LC is a broad notch.
ch1_7.seriesVoltageMagnification: And here comes the surprise. Even though the SUM of V_L and V_C across the pair is essentially zero at resonance (the two reactive voltages cancel), each one INDIVIDUALLY can be huge. The current at resonance is I = V_{\mathrm{in}} / R_{\mathrm{loss}}; the voltage across the inductor is V_L = I · X_L; combining gives V_L = V_{\mathrm{in}} · (X_L / R_{\mathrm{loss}}) = V_{\mathrm{in}} · Q. Push 10 V into a series LC with Q = 100 and you get 1000 V across each component — perfectly cancelling each other across the pair, but easily enough to arc across a small capacitor that wasn't rated for it. This is why tuned circuits handling high power need components rated several times the supply voltage.
ch1_7.seriesUseTrap: The series LC's natural job is the trap. Drop one across the signal path from line to ground and at f_0 it shorts that frequency away while passing everything else. Multi-band antennas use traps to make a single radiator behave as different lengths on different bands; TVI / RFI fixes routinely hang a series LC across the speaker leads of an audio system to drain a particular ham band away from the audio amplifier's input.
ch1_7.parallelIntro: Wire L and C across the same two nodes — both terminals shared — and the picture inverts. The two components now have the same voltage across them, and the currents through them are opposite (because L and C are 90° out of phase with the same voltage in opposite directions). At resonance, those two currents are equal in magnitude and opposite in sign — they cancel where they meet at the nodes, and the line current the source has to deliver drops to nearly zero. The internal currents around the LC loop, however, are still circulating — at the same magnitude as if the source were driving them directly. This «circulating tank current» is what gives the configuration its name.
ch1_7.parallelResonanceBehaviour: At f_0 a parallel LC looks like a near-open circuit. The source delivers almost no current — only enough to top up what the resistive losses dissipate each cycle. The impedance the source sees, R_P = Q · X_L, can be tens or hundreds of kilohms — even when the components themselves only have a few hundred ohms of reactance. Off-resonance the impedance falls sharply on either side, just as the series-LC current did, and at the same rate (set by Q).
ch1_7.parallelTankCurrent: Inside the tank, current still circulates. Q times the line current, in fact: a tank with Q = 100 driven by a 10 mA source carries 1 A circulating between L and C. The capacitor and inductor must each be rated to handle that current, even though the source only sees 10 mA. Old-school transmitter output stages with vacuum tubes routinely circulate amperes through tank coils that look small enough not to need it.
ch1_7.parallelUseTank: The parallel LC's natural job is the tuned circuit in receiver and transmitter front ends. Hang it between a transistor's collector and the supply rail, and the stage delivers maximum gain only at f_0 — every other frequency sees the tank's impedance fall and gets attenuated. Cascading several tanks (with the right coupling) gives the sharp-skirted band-pass of a superhet IF stage. Almost every receiver, transmitter, and antenna matching network you will meet has at least one parallel-tuned LC in it — usually several.
ch1_7.parallelDuality: Series and parallel LC are duals of one another. Same components, same f_0, same Q — but at resonance one looks like zero ohms and the other like infinite ohms. The voltage / current waveforms swap roles: where series shows current peak and voltage cancellation, parallel shows current cancellation and impedance peak. ARRL convention even calls the parallel case «antiresonance» to highlight the inversion. In practice, choose by what you want to do: pass a frequency through a path → series; reject one frequency from a path → series across to ground (trap); peak the gain of a stage at one frequency → parallel as the stage's load.
ch1_7.qIntro: Both LC topologies share the same defining number: how «sharp» the response is around <var>f_0</var>. Two equivalent ways to talk about sharpness:
ch1_7.qDefRatio: (1) The reactance-to-resistance ratio. Q = X_L / R_{\mathrm{loss}} for a series LC, or Q = R_P / X_L for a parallel LC. Both forms give the same number for the same physical pair of components — they are just two ways to look at the same loss model. The numerator is the energy-storing reactance at f_0; the denominator is the equivalent resistance that turns stored energy into heat. The ratio is dimensionless.
ch1_7.qDefBw: (2) The bandwidth ratio. Sweep the source through f_0 and find the two frequencies on either side where the response (current for series, impedance for parallel) has fallen to 0.707 of its peak — the «−3 dB» or half-power points. The frequency span between them is the bandwidth BW, and Q is just the resonant frequency divided by that bandwidth:
ch1_7.qBwExample: A 14 MHz tank with Q = 100 has a bandwidth of <nowrap>14 MHz / 100 = 140 kHz</nowrap>. Push Q to 1000 (a high-grade quartz crystal at the same frequency) and the bandwidth shrinks to 14 kHz — narrow enough to hear a single SSB voice transmission and reject everything else on the band. Drop Q to 10 (a deliberately broad tank) and the bandwidth opens to 1.4 MHz — wide enough to cover the entire 14 MHz amateur band without retuning.
ch1_7.qComponents: Where the loss comes from. In a hand-wound LC tank the inductor's winding resistance almost always dominates — copper has finite conductivity, and at HF and above, skin effect forces the current into the outer surface of the wire, raising its effective resistance roughly with √f. Capacitors of decent quality (mica, NP0 ceramic, vacuum) have Q in the thousands; even the dielectric losses of cheap ceramics rarely matter compared to coil losses below 30 MHz. The Q of the tank is approximately the Q of the inductor — pick the coil first.
ch1_7.qLoaded: Loaded vs unloaded Q. The Q we have been discussing is the unloaded Q (Q_U) — the bare tank's own losses, with nothing else connected. The instant you couple the tank to a source and a load, those connect through additional resistances that drain energy out of the tank — and the loaded Q (Q_L) is always lower than Q_U. As a rule of thumb, deliberately matching for half-power transfer drops Q_L to about half of Q_U. The bandwidth you actually measure on the bench is the loaded one; Q_U is what the component datasheet promises.
ch1_7.qWidgetIntro: The widget below lets you set <var>L</var>, <var>C</var>, and the loss resistance, and watch the response curve. Toggle between series and parallel modes — the two curves are mirror images. The −3 dB markers are drawn live, with <var>BW</var> and Q computed in the corner.
ch1_7.sectionScale: What L and C combinations land where
ch1_7.scaleIntro: It is worth building a feel for which L–C combinations land at which frequencies, because in radio you almost always pick components by glancing at a few familiar pairs rather than running the formula every time. The chart below shows a log frequency axis from MF (medium-frequency, ~1 MHz) to UHF (ultra-high-frequency, ~1 GHz), with example component pairs that resonate in each band — including the popular amateur bands marked along the bottom.
ch1_7.scaleNotes: Two patterns are worth memorising. First, the common amateur bands cluster around fairly small numbers: 100 pF and a few µH puts you in the HF region; 30 pF and 0.5 µH puts you on 2 m (the 144 MHz amateur band). Second, every decade of frequency requires either a 100× change in the L–C product, or a √100 = 10× change in each. Want to move a tank from 7 MHz to 14 MHz? Halve either component (one 10:1 variable capacitor swept across half its range will do it). Want to span from MF to VHF? You need different components, not just different positions on the same dial.
ch1_7.vnaIntro: The cleanest way to find the resonant frequency of a real LC tank is on a VNA. The VNA sweeps a range of frequencies, sends a small RF signal into your circuit at each one, and measures how much of the signal is reflected back. The reflected signal is reported as the S11 parameter (in dB) — a number that goes from 0 dB (everything reflected, the circuit looks open or shorted to the VNA) to large negative values (almost nothing reflected, the circuit absorbs the signal cleanly).
ch1_7.vnaCurveBehaviour: For a series LC across the VNA port, S11 dips sharply at f_0 — at resonance the LC looks like a near-short, the VNA's 50 Ω signal flows into it cleanly, and almost nothing reflects. The dip's depth tells you about losses; the dip's width tells you about Q. For a parallel LC across the port, S11 dips when the tank's impedance happens to match 50 Ω — which is generally NOT exactly at f_0 — but the impedance peak at f_0 still produces a recognisable feature on the trace. For first-pass measurements, the series-LC topology is easier to interpret.
ch1_7.vnaMarkers: Reading off the numbers. Set a marker on the dip's lowest point — that gives you f_0. Move two more markers either side until they read −3 dB up from the bottom — these are the f_L and f_H at the half-power points. Bandwidth is BW = f_H − f_L; Q is f_0 / BW. NanoVNA-Saver and most PC software for the VNA will mark these automatically when you ask for a «Q measurement» on a resonant feature.
ch1_7.vnaPracticeNote: Two practical notes. First, calibrate the VNA at the SMA connector you will use, not at the connector on the front of the unit — every adapter and bit of cable adds its own phase shift that the calibration must absorb. Second, for tanks with very high Q (say a quartz crystal at 10 MHz, Q = 50 000) the dip is so narrow that the VNA's default frequency resolution will skip right over it — you have to sweep a much narrower range with many more points to even SEE the resonance. The widget above lets you experiment with Q from 10 to 1000 and watch how the dip shape changes.
ch1_7.sectionApplications: Where you actually meet tuned circuits
ch1_7.appsIntro: The point of the chapter isn't the formula in itself — it's that LC resonance underlies every selective behaviour in radio. A short tour, working outward from the bench:
ch1_7.appsReceivers: Receivers. The front end of every superheterodyne receiver has a tuned circuit (or several) that select the desired band before the signal reaches the mixer. After the mixer, the IF strip is a chain of tuned circuits at a fixed intermediate frequency, set up to pass exactly the bandwidth of one signal and reject everything else. Direct-conversion and SDR receivers replace the IF chain with software but still need an LC pre-selector at the antenna.
ch1_7.appsTransmitters: Transmitters. The output stage of every analogue transmitter is built around a tuned LC tank between the active device and the antenna, both to peak the gain at the operating frequency and to filter out harmonics generated by the non-linear device. CW and SSB transmitters add band-pass filters at the IF stage for transmit-bandwidth shaping.
ch1_7.appsAntennas: Antennas. Every antenna is a distributed LC structure. A half-wave dipole has its self-inductance and self-capacitance arranged along its length so that the whole structure resonates at the band's centre frequency; SWR is lowest at f_0 and rises at the band edges. Antenna tuners (ATU) are explicit LC matching networks placed between the transmitter and a non-resonant antenna to make the transmitter see 50 Ω at the operating frequency, even when the antenna itself is presenting something quite different.
ch1_7.appsFilters: Filters. Chapter 1.8 will go deep into LC filter design — but every band-pass, band-stop, and many low- and high-pass filters in radio are LC structures whose component values are chosen to place specific resonances. The traps and tanks of this chapter are the building blocks.
ch1_7.keyTakeaway1: An inductor and a capacitor wired together oscillate at one specific frequency — the resonant frequency, f_0 = 1 / (2π√(LC)). The frequency is set by L and C alone; the picture is energy sloshing between the inductor's magnetic field and the capacitor's electric field, just as a swing trades kinetic and potential energy.
ch1_7.keyTakeaway2: Series LC at f_0: minimum impedance, maximum current, voltages on L and C cancel — but each one individually can be Q times the supply voltage. Used as the trap: a near-short across an unwanted frequency.
ch1_7.keyTakeaway3: Parallel LC at f_0: maximum impedance, minimum line current, but circulating tank current Q times the line current. Used as the tank: the gain-peaking load in receivers, transmitters, and matching networks. Series and parallel are duals — same components, same f_0, opposite behaviour.
ch1_7.keyTakeaway4: Q factor (quality factor) measures the sharpness of the resonance. Equivalent definitions: Q = X_L / R_{\mathrm{loss}} (reactance-to-loss ratio) and Q = f_0 / BW (resonant frequency over bandwidth). The inductor's winding losses almost always dominate; pick the coil first.
ch1_7.keyTakeaway5: On a VNA, a series LC across the port shows a clean S11 dip at f_0; markers at the −3 dB up-points give you BW and hence Q. Always calibrate at the connector you will actually use. The same workflow finds the resonance of any unknown LC, antenna, or filter you build.
ch1_7.labGoal: Build a parallel LC tank from the air-core coil you wound in chapter 1.6 and a known capacitor, sweep it on a NanoVNA, and read the resonant frequency and Q directly from the screen.
ch1_7.labEquip1: <vna>NanoVNA</vna> (or any VNA with a 1–500 MHz sweep range) and the open / short / load calibration standards that came with it.
ch1_7.labEquip2: <oscilloscope>Oscilloscope</oscilloscope> (optional, for cross-checking the resonance with a function generator if you don't have a VNA).
ch1_7.labEquip3: A small <breadboard>breadboard</breadboard> and short jumper wires.
ch1_7.labEquip4: A short coax pigtail with an SMA connector on one end and crocodile clips on the other (the «test fixture» — most NanoVNA kits include one).
ch1_7.labComp1: The air-core coil you wound in the chapter 1.6 lab — roughly 50 turns of magnet wire on a marker pen, around 300 µH.
ch1_7.labComp2: One 100 pF ceramic capacitor (NP0 / C0G dielectric, not Y5V — the dielectric stability matters at RF). 5 % tolerance is fine; 10 % is fine too but pick from a tighter batch if you have it.
ch1_7.labComp3: A 1 MΩ resistor (optional) — for damping the tank to a deliberately lower Q on the second sweep, so you can see the bandwidth widen.
ch1_7.labStep1: Predict the resonance. Plug your coil's measured inductance from chapter 1.6 (call it L, in microhenries) and the capacitor value (100 pF) into f_0 = 1 / (2π√(LC)). For L = 300 µH and C = 100 pF: f_0 ≈ 919 kHz. The exact prediction depends on your coil — write down the number you expect before turning on the VNA.
ch1_7.labStep2: Calibrate the VNA. Set the sweep range to span from one third to three times your predicted f_0 — for a 919 kHz target, sweep 300 kHz to 3 MHz. Run the open / short / load calibration at the end of your test fixture (the crocodile-clip end of the pigtail), not at the front-panel SMA — every centimetre of cable shifts the apparent resonance otherwise.
ch1_7.labStep3: Wire the parallel tank. On the breadboard, connect the coil and the 100 pF capacitor in parallel — both ends of the coil to both ends of the capacitor. Then connect the test-fixture clips across the parallel combination — one clip per node. Hit «sweep» on the VNA.
ch1_7.labStep4: Find f_0. The S11 trace should show a feature near your prediction — for a parallel LC with low loss the trace will rise toward 0 dB at f_0 (the tank reflects almost everything when it looks like a near-open) with a dip on either side. Place a marker at the highest point — that frequency is your measured f_0. Compare to your prediction; agreement within ±20 % is excellent for a hand-wound coil.
ch1_7.labStep5: Measure Q from the bandwidth. Drop two more markers at the points where the trace is 3 dB below the resonance peak (NOT the floor — the peak). Read off the two frequencies. BW = f_H − f_L, then Q = f_0 / BW. A typical air-core coil at sub-MHz frequencies gives Q in the 50–200 range, dominated by the wire's DC resistance plus a little dielectric loss in the breadboard.
ch1_7.labStep6: Damp the tank deliberately. Add the 1 MΩ resistor across the parallel LC. Sweep again. The resonant frequency should be essentially unchanged, but the peak should be lower and broader — Q has dropped because the resistor is dissipating energy each cycle. Repeat the bandwidth measurement and verify Q has fallen. Replace the 1 MΩ with progressively smaller values (100 kΩ, 10 kΩ) and watch Q collapse. This is exactly how a load coupled to a tank in a real radio reduces the loaded Q.
ch1_7.labExpected: A clear resonant feature on S11 within ±20 % of the predicted <var>f_0</var>; a measurable Q in the tens to a few hundred; the resonance shifts only slightly when you damp it, but the bandwidth opens noticeably with each smaller damping resistor.
ch1_7.labConnection: You have just done — at amateur scale and with one solder-free part — what every <ham>radio amateur</ham> does when checking an antenna: an S11 sweep, a marker on the dip, and a Q calculation. The same tools and the same numbers apply at every level of the hobby, from a 100 kHz LC trap to a microwave-band cavity filter.
ch1_7.labTrouble1: The trace is featureless across the sweep. Either the LC is way off the swept range (re-check your inductance value with the chapter 1.6 method), or one of the components has come unseated in the breadboard. Wiggle each component leg gently while watching the trace.
ch1_7.labTrouble2: The peak is at a much lower frequency than predicted. The most likely culprit is stray capacitance from the breadboard itself adding to the 100 pF — typical breadboards add 5–10 pF per row to ground. Either move to a faster surface (PCB or ugly construction) or include the stray in your prediction.
ch1_7.labTrouble3: The Q reading is impossibly high (Q > 500 with a hand-wound coil). You probably aren't actually at the −3 dB points — verify that the markers are 3 dB below the peak, not 3 dB up from the noise floor. The two are easy to confuse on a noisy trace.
ch1_7.quiz_q1: An LC tank consists of <var>L</var> = 10 µH and <var>C</var> = 100 pF. What is its approximate resonant frequency?
ch1_7.quiz_q1_explanation: <nowrap><var>f_0</var> = 1 / (2π√(<var>L</var><var>C</var>))</nowrap>. With <var>L</var> = 10 µH = 10⁻⁵ H and <var>C</var> = 100 pF = 10⁻¹⁰ F: <nowrap><var>L</var><var>C</var> = 10⁻¹⁵</nowrap>; <nowrap>√(<var>L</var><var>C</var>) = 3.16 × 10⁻⁸</nowrap>; <nowrap>2π√(<var>L</var><var>C</var>) ≈ 2 × 10⁻⁷</nowrap>; <nowrap><var>f_0</var> ≈ 5 × 10⁶ Hz = 5 MHz</nowrap>.
ch1_7.quiz_q2: You have an LC tank resonant at 14 MHz and you want to halve its resonant frequency to 7 MHz. By what factor must you multiply the capacitance (keeping <var>L</var> unchanged)?
ch1_7.quiz_q2_explanation: f_0 depends on the inverse square root of the LC product. To halve f_0 you have to quadruple the LC product, so quadrupling C alone (× 4) does the job. Doubling C alone would only drop f_0 by a factor of √2 ≈ 1.41.
ch1_7.quiz_q3: Which of the following best describes a parallel LC tank exactly at its resonant frequency?
ch1_7.quiz_q3_a: Minimum impedance, maximum line current, voltages on L and C cancel.
ch1_7.quiz_q3_b: Maximum impedance, minimum line current, large circulating current inside the tank.
ch1_7.quiz_q3_d: Acts as a near-short to all frequencies.
ch1_7.quiz_q3_explanation: A parallel LC at resonance presents its maximum impedance to the source, so the line current dips to a minimum. The tank current circulating between L and C, however, is Q times the line current — answer (a) describes a series LC instead.
ch1_7.quiz_q4: A 50 MHz LC tank has Q = 50. What is its bandwidth?
ch1_7.quiz_q5: A series LC at resonance is fed from a 10 V signal source. The Q of the LC pair is 100. What voltage appears across the inductor alone?
ch1_7.quiz_q5_explanation: At series resonance, the SUM of <var>V_L</var> and <var>V_C</var> across the pair is zero (they cancel) — but each one INDIVIDUALLY is Q times the input voltage. <nowrap><var>V_L</var> = Q × <var>V_{\mathrm{in}}</var> = 100 × 10 V = 1000 V</nowrap>. Components in high-Q tuned circuits must be rated for this voltage, not just the supply.
ch1_7.quiz_q6: Two LC tanks are designed for the same resonant frequency. Tank A uses 10 µH × 100 pF; tank B uses 100 µH × 10 pF. Which tank has the higher characteristic impedance <nowrap><var>Z_0</var> = √(<var>L</var>/<var>C</var>)</nowrap>?
ch1_7.quiz_q6_c: They are equal — the LC products are identical.
ch1_7.quiz_q6_d: Cannot be determined without knowing Q.
ch1_7.quiz_q6_explanation: Although both tanks have the same LC product (and therefore the same f_0), the L/C ratio is different. Tank A: Z_0 = √(10·10⁻⁶ / 100·10⁻¹²) = √(10⁵) ≈ 316 Ω. Tank B: Z_0 = √(100·10⁻⁶ / 10·10⁻¹²) = √(10⁷) ≈ 3160 Ω. Tank B has the higher characteristic impedance — by a factor of 10.
ch1_7.quiz_q7: On a VNA sweep of a series LC across the port, you see an S11 dip at 7.2 MHz. The −3 dB up markers either side of the dip read 7.140 MHz and 7.260 MHz. What is the Q of the LC?
ch1_7.quiz_q8: An air-core LC tank measures Q = 200 unloaded on a VNA. You couple it to a load via a small capacitor and re-measure: Q has dropped to 100. What does this tell you?
ch1_7.quiz_q8_a: The tank is broken — Q should not change.
ch1_7.quiz_q8_b: The load is dissipating energy each cycle, lowering the loaded Q below the unloaded Q. Bandwidth has doubled accordingly.
ch1_7.quiz_q8_c: The resonant frequency has shifted; Q is unchanged.
ch1_7.quiz_q8_explanation: Coupling a load to a tank always lowers Q — the load draws energy that the tank then has to top up each cycle, which is exactly what loss does. The drop from 200 to 100 means the bandwidth has doubled (<nowrap><var>BW</var> = <var>f_0</var> / Q</nowrap>); the resonant frequency itself barely shifts. This is why receiver designers carefully match the IF strip's loading: too tight a coupling collapses the selectivity.

calc.title: Resonance frequency calculator
calc.description: Set L and C to compute f₀ = 1 / (2π√LC). The widget also reports the period T = 1/f₀ and the characteristic impedance Z₀ = √(L/C) — the value of X_L = X_C at resonance.
calc.hint: Try L = 4 µH and C = 130 pF — that pair lands very close to 7 MHz, on the 40-metre amateur band. Doubling either one drops f₀ by √2; quadrupling halves it.

response.title: LC response curve — Q and bandwidth
response.description: Pick L, C and the loss resistance, then watch the response around f₀. Toggle between series (current vs frequency) and parallel (impedance vs frequency); the −3 dB markers and Q are computed live.
response.hint: Drop R toward 1 Ω to watch the curve sharpen (high Q, narrow BW); raise it past 100 Ω and the resonance broadens until it almost disappears. The two modes are mirror images — same f₀ and Q, opposite peak direction.

scale.title: What L–C combinations land where
scale.description: Frequency on the horizontal axis (logarithmic). Each marker shows an example L–C pair and the amateur or commercial band it matches.
scale.ariaLabel: Logarithmic frequency axis from 100 kHz to 1 GHz, with example LC component pairs marked above each ham band.

vna.title: S11 dip on a VNA sweep — finding f₀ and Q
vna.description: A simulated VNA screen showing the S11 magnitude curve of a series LC near resonance. Three markers: f₀ at the dip, plus f_L and f_H at the −3 dB up-points. Adjust Q to see the dip sharpen or broaden.
vna.hint: On a real NanoVNA, the same trace appears for any series LC across the port. The dip's depth tells you about loss; the dip's width tells you about Q.

ch1_7.schematicSeriesAria: Schematic of a series LC: a signal source on the left feeds an inductor L and a capacitor C wired end-to-end; the loop closes back to the source through ground.
ch1_7.schematicSeriesCaption: Series LC. Source feeds L and C in a single loop. At f_0 the impedance the source sees is minimum (a near-short); off-resonance it rises sharply.
ch1_7.schematicParallelAria: Schematic of a parallel LC tank: the inductor L and capacitor C are wired between the same two nodes, with the source connecting to those nodes from the left.
ch1_7.schematicParallelCaption: Parallel LC (tank). Both components share the same two nodes. At f_0 the impedance is maximum (a near-open); circulating current inside the tank is Q times the line current.
